questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the final product $\mathrm{P_3}$ in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The starting material is 3,3-dimethylbut-1-ene, $(CH_3)_3C-CH=CH_2$.

The reaction with $HCl$ begins with the electrophilic addition of a proton to form a secondary carbocation: $(CH_3)_3C-C^+H-CH_3$.

This carbocation undergoes a 1,2-methyl shift to form a more stable tertiary carbocation: $(CH_3)_2C^+-CH(CH_3)_2$.

Nucleophilic attack by the chloride ion on this rearranged carbocation yields $P_1$, 2-chloro-2,3-dimethylbutane, $(CH_3)_2C(Cl)-CH(CH_3)_2$.

Treatment of $P_1$ with alcoholic $KOH$ leads to E2 dehydrohalogenation. According to Saytzeff's rule, the more highly substituted alkene is formed as the major product, giving 2,3-dimethylbut-2-ene, $(CH_3)_2C=C(CH_3)_2$, as $P_2$.

Reaction of $P_2$ with m-CPBA causes epoxidation of the double bond, producing 2,2,3,3-tetramethyloxirane.

The epoxide undergoes a ring-opening reaction upon treatment with the Grignard reagent $CH_3MgBr$. The effective addition of a methyl group to one of the fully substituted carbons, accompanied by ring opening and subsequent hydrolysis with $H_2O$, gives the corresponding tertiary alcohol.

The carbon receiving the methyl group transforms from $-C(CH_3)_2-$ to a tert-butyl group, $-C(CH_3)_3$. The other carbon of the epoxide ring retains the oxygen, becoming $-C(OH)(CH_3)_2$ after hydrolysis.

Therefore, the final product $P_3$ is 2,3,3-trimethylbutan-2-ol, $(CH_3)_3C-C(OH)(CH_3)_2$.

This structural formula matches the compound shown in option (2), which features a tert-butyl group attached to a carbon bonded to two methyl groups and a hydroxyl group.

Answer :

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following halogenating agents in decreasing order of their selectivity in free radical reactions:
(i) NBS
(ii) NCS
(iii) $\mathrm{Cl}_2$
(iv) $\mathrm{F}_2$
questions.solution:
According to the reactivity-selectivity principle, a less reactive free radical is more selective in its reactions.

The reactivity of halogen radicals follows the order: $\mathrm{F}^{\bullet} > \mathrm{Cl}^{\bullet} > \mathrm{Br}^{\bullet}$.

Consequently, the selectivity follows the reverse order: bromination is highly selective, chlorination is moderately selective, and fluorination is the least selective (almost unselective due to its explosive reactivity).

NBS (N-bromosuccinimide) acts as a source of bromine radicals ($\mathrm{Br}^{\bullet}$) in low concentrations, making it highly selective, especially for allylic and benzylic positions.

NCS (N-chlorosuccinimide) acts as a source of chlorine radicals ($\mathrm{Cl}^{\bullet}$). It is less selective than NBS but more selective than direct chlorination using $\mathrm{Cl}_2$ because the controlled, low concentration of $\mathrm{Cl}_2$ minimizes side reactions such as electrophilic addition.

$\mathrm{F}_2$ is the most reactive and therefore the least selective halogenating agent.

Thus, the decreasing order of selectivity is NBS > NCS > $\mathrm{Cl}_2$ > $\mathrm{F}_2$, which corresponds to (i) > (ii) > (iii) > (iv).

Answer: (i) > (ii) > (iii) > (iv)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product (B) in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The given starting material is 1,2-dibromocyclopentane.

When it reacts with 1 equivalent of magnesium (Mg) in the presence of dry ether, it forms a mono-Grignard reagent. Magnesium inserts into one of the carbon-bromine bonds to form 2-bromocyclopentylmagnesium bromide. This is intermediate A.

Upon heating ($\Delta$), this intermediate undergoes an intramolecular elimination reaction. The carbon atom bonded to magnesium has a partial negative charge (carbanion character) and acts as an internal nucleophile. It donates its electron pair to form a carbon-carbon double bond, simultaneously expelling the adjacent bromide ion as a leaving group.

This elimination of $MgBr_2$ results in the formation of cyclopentene as the major product B.

Therefore, product B is cyclopentene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The reaction $2 \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{Cl} + \mathrm{Zn} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{3} + \mathrm{ZnCl}_{2}$ is known as:
questions.solution:
The reaction of an alkyl halide with zinc metal to form a symmetrical alkane is known as the Frankland reaction.

$2 \mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{Cl} + \mathrm{Zn} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{3} + \mathrm{ZnCl}_{2}$

Wurtz reaction involves the use of sodium metal instead of zinc.

Fittig reaction involves aryl halides and sodium.

Wurtz-Fittig reaction involves a mixture of alkyl and aryl halides with sodium.

Answer: Frankland reaction

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major final product $\text{B}$ of the following reaction sequence.
questions.solution:
The reactant is $(2S, 3S)-3-deuteriobutan-2-ol$.

Reaction with $SOCl_2$ and $(CH_3)_3N$ proceeds via an $S_N2$ mechanism, causing inversion of configuration at C2 to form $(2R, 3S)-2-chloro-3-deuteriobutane$ (Compound A).

Treatment with the strong, bulky base $t-BuO^-$ leads to an E2 elimination, which requires an anti-periplanar arrangement of the leaving group ($Cl$) and the $\beta$-proton/deuteron.

From the most stable conformer of Compound A, anti-elimination of $D$ and $Cl$ yields $(E)-2-butene$. Alternatively, rotation to a less stable conformer allows anti-elimination of $H$ and $Cl$, yielding $(Z)-2-deuterio-2-butene$.

Due to the primary kinetic isotope effect ($k_H > k_D$), the cleavage of the $C-H$ bond is significantly faster than the $C-D$ bond. This kinetic preference overcomes the slight steric disadvantage of the required conformation.

Therefore, the major product is $(Z)-2-deuterio-2-butene$, which retains the deuterium atom and corresponds to option (3).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds is most reactive towards the $\mathrm{E1cB}$ elimination reaction?
questions.solution:
The E1cB elimination reaction proceeds via a two-step mechanism involving the formation of a carbanion intermediate. The rate of the reaction is determined by the stability of this carbanion, which is formed by the abstraction of a $\beta$-proton by a base.

In option (A), abstraction of the proton from the $sp^3$ carbon of the cyclopentadiene ring generates a cyclopentadienyl anion. This intermediate is planar, fully conjugated, and possesses $6\pi$ electrons, making it aromatic and highly stable.

In option (B), abstraction of the proton generates a cycloheptatrienyl anion. This intermediate possesses $8\pi$ electrons, making it antiaromatic and highly unstable.

In option (C), the resulting primary carbanion lacks resonance stabilization.

In option (D), the resulting benzylic carbanion is resonance stabilized by the phenyl ring but is less stable than the aromatic cyclopentadienyl anion.

Because compound (A) forms the most stable carbanion intermediate, it exhibits the highest reactivity towards the E1cB elimination reaction.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product $\mathrm{B}$ in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The starting material, 4-bromocyclohexylmethanamine, contains both a nucleophilic primary amine group and an electrophilic secondary bromide. In the presence of dilute $\mathrm{OH^-}$, the amine nitrogen undergoes an intramolecular $S_N2$ reaction, attacking the C4 carbon and displacing the bromide ion. This cyclization forms the bicyclic intermediate A, 2-azabicyclo[2.2.2]octane.

Treatment of intermediate A with excess $\mathrm{CH_3I}$ results in exhaustive methylation, converting the amine into a quaternary ammonium iodide salt. Subsequent reaction with moist $\mathrm{Ag_2O}$ replaces the iodide with a hydroxide ion, forming a quaternary ammonium hydroxide suitable for Hofmann elimination upon heating ($\Delta$).

During the Hofmann elimination, the hydroxide base abstracts a $\beta$-hydrogen to form a double bond, accompanied by the cleavage of a C-N bond. The quaternary nitrogen is bonded to a tertiary ring carbon (C4) and a secondary exocyclic carbon. There are two possible elimination pathways:

First, abstraction of the tertiary $\beta$-hydrogen at the C1 bridgehead position cleaves the exocyclic C-N bond, yielding an exocyclic double bond to form N,N-dimethyl-4-methylenecyclohexan-1-amine.

Second, abstraction of a secondary $\beta$-hydrogen at the C3 or C5 position of the ring cleaves the endocyclic C-N bond, yielding an endocyclic double bond to form 4-(dimethylaminomethyl)cyclohexene.

Hofmann elimination favors the abstraction of the most acidic and least sterically hindered $\beta$-hydrogen. The secondary hydrogens at C3 and C5 are more acidic and less hindered than the tertiary bridgehead hydrogen at C1. Furthermore, there are four equivalent secondary $\beta$-hydrogens compared to only one tertiary $\beta$-hydrogen, statistically favoring the second pathway. The formation of the endocyclic alkene is also thermodynamically more stable than the exocyclic alkene. Consequently, cleavage of the endocyclic C-N bond predominates, producing 4-(dimethylaminomethyl)cyclohexene as the major product B.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product $\mathrm{B}$ in the following two-step reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The given starting material is cyclopent-3-en-1-yl tosylate.

In the first step, reaction with alcoholic KOH (a strong base) causes an E2 elimination of the tosylate group (-OTs) along with an adjacent proton. This elimination yields the conjugated diene 1,3-cyclopentadiene as product A.

In the second step, bromoform ($CHBr_3$) reacts with the strong base tert-butoxide ($t-BuO^-$) to undergo $\alpha$-elimination, generating the highly reactive electrophilic intermediate dibromocarbene ($:CBr_2$).

$CHBr_3 + t-BuO^- \rightarrow :CBr_2 + t-BuOH + Br^-$

The dibromocarbene undergoes a [2+1] cycloaddition with one of the double bonds of 1,3-cyclopentadiene to form a bicyclic intermediate, 6,6-dibromobicyclo[3.1.0]hex-2-ene.

Under the strongly basic conditions, this intermediate undergoes a ring expansion. The base abstracts an allylic proton from the five-membered ring, which triggers the electrocyclic opening of the fused cyclopropane ring and the simultaneous expulsion of a bromide ion ($Br^-$).

This rearrangement is driven by aromatization, leading to the formation of a stable, six-membered aromatic ring. The final major product B is bromobenzene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of their rate of hydrolysis via the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ mechanism:

I.
II.
III.
IV.
questions.solution:
The rate of hydrolysis via the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ mechanism depends on the stability of the intermediate carbocation formed after the departure of the bromide ion.

The carbocations formed are benzyl cation (I) and its para-alkyl substituted derivatives (II, III, IV). All are stabilized by resonance with the benzene ring. The para-alkyl groups provide additional stabilization through hyperconjugation (+H) and inductive (+I) effects.

For alkyl groups attached to a benzene ring, hyperconjugation dominates over the inductive effect. The extent of hyperconjugation is directly proportional to the number of $\alpha$-hydrogens on the alkyl group attached to the ring:
Compound II ($-CH_3$ group): $3$ $\alpha$-hydrogens
Compound III ($-CH_2CH_3$ group): $2$ $\alpha$-hydrogens
Compound IV ($-CH(CH_3)_2$ group): $1$ $\alpha$-hydrogen
Compound I (no alkyl group): $0$ $\alpha$-hydrogens

The electron-donating effect via hyperconjugation decreases in the order: $-CH_3 > -CH_2CH_3 > -CH(CH_3)_2$.

Thus, the stability of the carbocations follows the order: II > III > IV > I.

Therefore, the rate of $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ hydrolysis follows the same order: II > III > IV > I.

Answer: II > III > IV > I

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the final product $\mathrm{C}$ in the given reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
Reaction of cyclopentene with NBS in the presence of light ($h\nu$) leads to allylic bromination, forming 3-bromocyclopentene as compound A.

Treatment of 3-bromocyclopentene (A) with alcoholic KOH causes dehydrohalogenation (E2 elimination), yielding 1,3-cyclopentadiene as compound B.

1,3-cyclopentadiene contains highly acidic methylene protons at the $sp^3$ hybridized carbon because the removal of a proton generates the cyclopentadienyl anion, which is highly stable due to being aromatic (satisfying Hückel's rule with 6 $\pi$ electrons).

When 1,3-cyclopentadiene (B) reacts with methylmagnesium bromide ($\text{CH}_3\text{MgBr}$), an acid-base reaction takes place. The Grignard reagent acts as a strong base, abstracting an acidic proton to form methane gas ($\text{CH}_4$) and cyclopentadienyl magnesium bromide.

The final products are methane and cyclopentadienyl magnesium bromide. Since none of the given structures match these products, the correct choice is 'None of these'.

Answer: None of these

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An aromatic compound (A) with the molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{7}\mathrm{H}_{6}\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ gives a precipitate of $\mathrm{AgCl}$ on boiling with an alcoholic $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ solution, and yields a compound of formula $\mathrm{C}_{7}\mathrm{H}_{7}\mathrm{OCl}$ on treatment with aqueous sodium hydroxide. Oxidation of (A) gives a monochlorobenzoic acid which affords only one mononitro derivative upon nitration. Compound (A) is:
questions.solution:
Compound (A) has the molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{7}\mathrm{H}_{6}\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$.

It gives a precipitate of $\mathrm{AgCl}$ on boiling with alcoholic $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ solution, which indicates the presence of a benzylic chlorine atom (reactive towards nucleophilic substitution).

Treatment with aqueous $\mathrm{NaOH}$ yields a compound with the formula $\mathrm{C}_{7}\mathrm{H}_{7}\mathrm{OCl}$. This means only one chlorine atom is replaced by an $-\mathrm{OH}$ group, confirming that one chlorine is benzylic and the other is attached directly to the benzene ring (aryl chlorine, which is unreactive under these conditions). Thus, (A) is a chlorobenzyl chloride.

Oxidation of (A) gives a monochlorobenzoic acid. The position of the chlorine atom on the ring remains unchanged.

Nitration of this monochlorobenzoic acid yields only one mononitro derivative. Let us analyze the possible isomers:
1. $o$-chlorobenzoic acid: The $-\mathrm{COOH}$ group directs to the meta positions (3 and 5), and the $-\mathrm{Cl}$ group directs to the ortho/para positions (3 and 5). Since positions 3 and 5 are not equivalent, nitration gives a mixture of two products.
2. $m$-chlorobenzoic acid: The directing effects of $-\mathrm{COOH}$ and $-\mathrm{Cl}$ lead to multiple non-equivalent positions, resulting in a mixture of products.
3. $p$-chlorobenzoic acid: The $-\mathrm{COOH}$ group directs to positions 3 and 5. The $-\mathrm{Cl}$ group also directs to positions 3 and 5 (ortho to $-\mathrm{Cl}$). Because the molecule has a plane of symmetry passing through C1 and C4, positions 3 and 5 are identical. Therefore, nitration yields only one product (4-chloro-3-nitrobenzoic acid).

Since only $p$-chlorobenzoic acid gives a single mononitro derivative, the original compound (A) must be $p$-chlorobenzyl chloride.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction involves the treatment of a vicinal dibromide with sodium iodide in acetone, which undergoes a stereospecific elimination to form an alkene. The mechanism requires the two leaving bromine atoms to be in an antiperiplanar conformation.

To determine the stereochemistry of the product, we analyze the Fischer projection. A reliable method is to perform an even number of position swaps on the chiral centers to place both leaving groups on the vertical axis.

For the top chiral carbon, perform two swaps to move the bromine atom to the top vertical position. Swapping $\text{Br}$ with $\text{Ph}$, and then $\text{Ph}$ with $\text{H}$, places $\text{Br}$ at the top, $\text{Ph}$ on the left, and $\text{H}$ on the right. This even number of swaps preserves the original configuration.

For the bottom chiral carbon, the bromine atom is already at the bottom vertical position. The $\text{Ph}$ group is on the left, and the $\text{CH}_3$ group is on the right.

In this modified Fischer projection, both $\text{Br}$ atoms are on the vertical axis. For this type of elimination, the substituents on the same side of the vertical axis in the Fischer projection will end up trans to each other in the resulting alkene.

Both $\text{Ph}$ groups are on the left side of the projection. Therefore, they will be trans to each other across the newly formed double bond. Similarly, the $\text{H}$ and $\text{CH}_3$ groups on the right side will be trans to each other.

The resulting product is an alkene where the two phenyl groups are trans. This geometry matches the structure in option (4).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product [B] in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The given reaction sequence involves two main steps:

1. Substitution Reaction ($S_N2$): The first step is the reaction of the chiral alkyl bromide with $NaN_3$ in DMF. DMF (Dimethylformamide) is a polar aprotic solvent, which strongly favors the $S_N2$ mechanism. In an $S_N2$ reaction, the nucleophile ($N_3^-$) attacks the electrophilic carbon from the side opposite to the leaving group ($Br^-$), resulting in an inversion of configuration at that specific chiral center (C2).
In the given Fischer projection, the $-Br$ group is on the right side. After the $S_N2$ attack, the incoming $-N_3$ group will be positioned on the left side, and the $-H$ atom will be pushed to the right side. The configuration at the other chiral center (C3) remains completely unaffected. This forms the intermediate alkyl azide [A].

2. Reduction Reaction: The second step involves the reduction of the intermediate azide [A] using $LiAlH_4$ followed by hydrolysis ($H_2O$). Lithium aluminum hydride ($LiAlH_4$) is a strong reducing agent that reduces azides ($R-N_3$) to primary amines ($R-NH_2$). This reduction process does not break any bonds connected directly to the chiral center, so the stereochemical configuration at C2 is retained as it was in [A].

Therefore, in the final major product [B], the $-NH_2$ group will be on the left side and the $-H$ atom will be on the right side at C2, while the C3 stereocenter remains unchanged with $-D$ on the left and $-H$ on the right.

Comparing this derived structure with the given options, it perfectly matches Option (B).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following alkyl bromides in decreasing order of their reactivity towards nucleophilic substitution with alcoholic $\mathrm{AgCN}$:

(I) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{Br}$

(II)

(III)
questions.solution:
The reaction of alkyl halides with alcoholic $\mathrm{AgCN}$ predominantly follows the $S_N1$ mechanism. This is because the $\mathrm{Ag}^+$ ion acts as a Lewis acid, coordinating with the leaving halogen atom and facilitating the cleavage of the $\mathrm{C-Br}$ bond to form a carbocation intermediate (along with the precipitation of $\mathrm{AgBr}$).

The reactivity of an alkyl halide in an $S_N1$ reaction is directly proportional to the stability of the carbocation formed.

The carbocations generated from the given alkyl bromides are:
(I) $\mathrm{CH_3-CH_2-Br} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH_3-CH_2^+}$ ($1^\circ$ carbocation)
(II) $\mathrm{CH_3-CH(CH_3)-Br} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH_3-CH^+-CH_3}$ ($2^\circ$ carbocation)
(III) $\mathrm{CH_3-C(CH_3)_2-Br} \rightarrow \mathrm{(CH_3)_3C^+}$ ($3^\circ$ carbocation)

The order of stability of carbocations is $3^\circ > 2^\circ > 1^\circ$ due to the +I effect and hyperconjugation of the attached alkyl groups.

Therefore, the decreasing order of reactivity towards alcoholic $\mathrm{AgCN}$ is (III) > (II) > (I).

Answer: (III) > (II) > (I)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following statements are correct for the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction of alkyl halides?
I. Both the alkyl halide and the nucleophile are involved in the transition state.
II. The reaction proceeds with inversion of configuration at the substitution centre.
III. The reaction proceeds with retention of configuration at the substitution centre.
IV. The order of reactivity of alkyl halides is $3^{\circ} > 2^{\circ} > 1^{\circ}$.
V. The nucleophile must have an unshared electron pair and bear a negative charge.
VI. The greater the nucleophilicity of the nucleophile, the greater the rate of the reaction.
questions.solution:
Statement I is correct. The $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction is a concerted, single-step process where the transition state involves both the alkyl halide and the nucleophile.

Statement II is correct. The nucleophile attacks from the backside of the leaving group, leading to an inversion of configuration at the substitution centre.

Statement III is incorrect. The reaction proceeds with inversion, not retention.

Statement IV is incorrect. Due to steric hindrance, the reactivity order of alkyl halides in $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reactions is $1^{\circ} > 2^{\circ} > 3^{\circ}$.

Statement V is incorrect. A nucleophile must have an unshared electron pair, but it can be either neutral (e.g., $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$, $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$) or negatively charged.

Statement VI is correct. Since the nucleophile is involved in the rate-determining step, a stronger nucleophile increases the rate of the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction.

Therefore, only statements I, II, and VI are correct.

Answer: I, II and VI

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following two stereoisomers of 1-bromo-3-tert-butyl-5-methylcyclohexane:

1:
2:

Choose the correct statement(s) concerning their reaction rates:
I. 1 will undergo $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reactions faster than 2.
II. 1 will undergo E1 reactions faster than 2.
III. 1 and 2 will undergo $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reactions at the same rate.
questions.solution:
The rate of $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ and $\mathrm{E}1$ reactions depends on the rate-determining step, which is the formation of a carbocation intermediate via the departure of the leaving group ($\mathrm{Br}^{-}$). The reaction rate is higher if the ground state of the reactant is less stable (higher in energy) due to steric strain that is relieved upon ionization, a phenomenon known as steric acceleration.

First, let's determine the most stable chair conformation for both isomers. The bulky tert-butyl group will lock the cyclohexane ring in a conformation where it occupies an equatorial position to avoid severe steric repulsions.

In a cyclohexane chair, at the 1, 3, and 5 positions, the axial bonds point in the same direction (either all up or all down), and the equatorial bonds point in the opposite direction. Let's assume a chair conformation where the 'up' bonds at positions 1, 3, and 5 are equatorial, and the 'down' bonds are axial.

For Isomer 1:
The tert-butyl group is wedge (up), so it is equatorial.
The $\mathrm{Br}$ atom is dash (down), so it is axial.
The methyl group is dash (down), so it is also axial.
Because both $\mathrm{Br}$ and the methyl group are axial and on the same side of the ring, they experience a severe 1,3-diaxial interaction. This makes the ground state of Isomer 1 highly unstable (high energy).

For Isomer 2:
The tert-butyl group is wedge (up), so it is equatorial.
The $\mathrm{Br}$ atom is wedge (up), so it is equatorial.
The methyl group is dash (down), so it is axial.
Here, $\mathrm{Br}$ is equatorial, so there is no 1,3-diaxial interaction between $\mathrm{Br}$ and the methyl group. Thus, Isomer 2 is more stable (lower energy) than Isomer 1.

When the $\mathrm{Br}^{-}$ leaves to form a carbocation, the carbon atom becomes $sp^2$ hybridized and planar. This relieves the severe 1,3-diaxial strain present in Isomer 1. Because Isomer 1 has a higher ground state energy but forms a similar carbocation intermediate (and transition state) as Isomer 2, the activation energy ($E_a$) for the ionization of Isomer 1 is significantly lower.

Therefore, Isomer 1 will undergo both $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ and $\mathrm{E}1$ reactions faster than Isomer 2.

Statements I and II are correct.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ solvolysis of the following halides in aqueous formic acid:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
(iv)

Which of the following represents the correct decreasing order of their reactivity?
questions.solution:
The rate of $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ solvolysis depends on the stability of the carbocation intermediate formed in the rate-determining step. The more stable the carbocation, the faster the reaction.

Let us analyze the carbocations formed by the departure of the bromide ion from each compound:

For (i), the departure of $\mathrm{Br}^{-}$ yields a secondary alkyl carbocation, $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})-\mathrm{CH}^{+}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$. This carbocation is stabilized by hyperconjugation with $4$ $\alpha$-hydrogens.

For (ii), the departure of $\mathrm{Br}^{-}$ yields a tertiary alkyl carbocation ($1$-methylcyclohexyl cation). This carbocation is stabilized by hyperconjugation with $7$ $\alpha$-hydrogens.

For (iii), the departure of $\mathrm{Br}^{-}$ yields a diphenylmethyl carbocation, $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{CH}^{+}-\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}$. This carbocation is highly stabilized by extensive resonance delocalization of the positive charge over two benzene rings.

For (iv), the departure of $\mathrm{Br}^{-}$ yields a secondary benzylic carbocation ($1$-tetralyl cation). This carbocation is stabilized by resonance with one benzene ring as well as hyperconjugation from the adjacent $\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ group.

Comparing the stabilities of these carbocations:
The diphenylmethyl cation (iii) is the most stable due to resonance with two phenyl rings.
The secondary benzylic cation (iv) is more stable than the tertiary alkyl cation (ii) because resonance stabilization by a phenyl ring generally outweighs the hyperconjugative stabilization of a tertiary carbon in solvolysis conditions.
The tertiary alkyl cation (ii) is more stable than the secondary alkyl cation (i) due to a greater number of $\alpha$-hydrogens and $+\mathrm{I}$ effect.

Therefore, the decreasing order of stability of the carbocations is (iii) > (iv) > (ii) > (i).

This corresponds exactly to the decreasing order of reactivity of the given halides in $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ solvolysis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the correct Fischer projection for the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
Let us determine the absolute configuration of the reactant.
The priorities of the groups attached to the chiral center are: $-Br$ (1) > $-Et$ (2) > $-Me$ (3) > $-H$ (4).
In the given 3D representation, the lowest priority group ($-H$) is on a dashed bond, meaning it is pointing away from the viewer.
Looking at the remaining groups: $-Br$ is pointing down-right, $-Et$ is pointing down-left (on a wedge), and $-Me$ is pointing straight up.
The sequence from priority 1 $\rightarrow$ 2 $\rightarrow$ 3 is $-Br \rightarrow -Et \rightarrow -Me$, which traces a clockwise direction.
Since the lowest priority group is pointing away, the clockwise direction indicates an (R) configuration.
Thus, the reactant is (R)-2-bromobutane.

The reaction with $NaN_3$ in DMF (a polar aprotic solvent) proceeds via an $S_N2$ mechanism.
The $S_N2$ reaction involves inversion of configuration at the chiral center.
Therefore, the major product will have the (S) configuration.
The nucleophile $-N_3$ replaces the leaving group $-Br$.
The priorities in the product are: $-N_3$ (1) > $-Et$ (2) > $-Me$ (3) > $-H$ (4).

Now, let us determine the configuration of the Fischer projections given in the options:
Option (A): $-N_3$ is top, $-H$ is bottom, $-Me$ is left, $-Et$ is right.
The lowest priority group ($-H$) is on a vertical bond (pointing away).
The sequence 1 ($-N_3$) $\rightarrow$ 2 ($-Et$) $\rightarrow$ 3 ($-Me$) is clockwise.
This corresponds to the (R) configuration.

Option (B): $-Me$ is top, $-Et$ is bottom, $-H$ is left, $-N_3$ is right.
The lowest priority group ($-H$) is on a horizontal bond (pointing towards the viewer).
The sequence 1 ($-N_3$) $\rightarrow$ 2 ($-Et$) $\rightarrow$ 3 ($-Me$) is clockwise.
Since $-H$ is horizontal, the actual configuration is the opposite, which is (S).

Option (C): $-Me$ is top, $-Et$ is bottom, $-N_3$ is left, $-H$ is right.
The lowest priority group ($-H$) is on a horizontal bond.
The sequence 1 ($-N_3$) $\rightarrow$ 2 ($-Et$) $\rightarrow$ 3 ($-Me$) is counter-clockwise.
Since $-H$ is horizontal, the actual configuration is (R).

Option (D): $-N_3$ is top, $-Me$ is bottom, $-Et$ is left, $-H$ is right.
The lowest priority group ($-H$) is on a horizontal bond.
The sequence 1 ($-N_3$) $\rightarrow$ 2 ($-Et$) $\rightarrow$ 3 ($-Me$) is counter-clockwise.
Since $-H$ is horizontal, the actual configuration is (R).

The correct Fischer projection for the (S) product is given in Option (B).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the given reactions are likely to proceed predominantly via an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ mechanism?

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
(iv)
questions.solution:
To determine which reactions proceed predominantly via an $\text{S}_{\text{N}}1$ mechanism, we need to analyze the substrate (alkyl halide) and the reaction conditions (solvent and nucleophile) for each case.

1. Reaction (i): The reactant is (chloromethyl)cyclohexane, which is a primary alkyl halide. The reagents are NaI in acetone. Acetone is a polar aprotic solvent, which strongly favors the $\text{S}_{\text{N}}2$ mechanism. Primary halides do not form stable carbocations and thus do not undergo $\text{S}_{\text{N}}1$ reactions. Therefore, this reaction proceeds via an $\text{S}_{\text{N}}2$ mechanism.

2. Reaction (ii): The reactant has both a primary chloride ($-\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}$) and a secondary chloride (on the ring). The reagents are NaI in acetone. This is the classic Finkelstein reaction condition. The polar aprotic solvent (acetone) and a good nucleophile (I$^-$) dictate an $\text{S}_{\text{N}}2$ mechanism.

3. Reaction (iii): The reactant is the same as in (ii), possessing both a primary and a secondary chloride. The reaction is carried out in methanol (MeOH), which is a polar protic solvent. Polar protic solvents stabilize carbocations and favor the $\text{S}_{\text{N}}1$ mechanism. Furthermore, the product shows substitution at the secondary carbon rather than the less hindered primary carbon. In an $\text{S}_{\text{N}}2$ reaction, the primary chloride would react much faster. The observed regioselectivity (reaction at the secondary site) indicates that the reaction proceeds via the more stable secondary carbocation, confirming an $\text{S}_{\text{N}}1$ mechanism.

4. Reaction (iv): The reactant is 1-chloro-1-methylcyclohexane, which is a tertiary alkyl halide. Tertiary halides are too sterically hindered to undergo $\text{S}_{\text{N}}2$ reactions. In the presence of a polar protic solvent like methanol, they readily ionize to form a stable tertiary carbocation. Therefore, this reaction proceeds exclusively via an $\text{S}_{\text{N}}1$ mechanism.

Based on this analysis, reactions (iii) and (iv) are likely to proceed predominantly via an $\text{S}_{\text{N}}1$ mechanism.

Answer: (iii) and (iv)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of reactivity for the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction among the following compounds:

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
The reactivity of a compound in an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction depends on the stability of the intermediate carbocation formed after the departure of the leaving group ($\mathrm{Br}^{-}$).

1. Compound (I) forms the cyclopentyl cation. This is a secondary, non-aromatic carbocation and is relatively stable.

2. Compound (II) forms the cyclopentadienyl cation. This cation has a continuous cyclic conjugated system with $4\pi$ electrons. According to Hückel's rule, it is antiaromatic and therefore highly unstable.

3. Compound (III) forms the 5-methylcyclopentadienyl cation. Like the cation from (II), it has $4\pi$ electrons and is antiaromatic. However, the presence of the electron-donating methyl group provides slight stabilization through the inductive effect ($+\mathrm{I}$) and hyperconjugation, making it less unstable than the unsubstituted cyclopentadienyl cation.

Since non-aromatic carbocations are significantly more stable than antiaromatic carbocations, compound (I) is the most reactive. Between the two antiaromatic carbocations, the one derived from (III) is slightly more stable than the one from (II).

Therefore, the correct order of reactivity for the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction is (I) > (III) > (II).

Answer: (I) > (III) > (II)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following substrates will undergo maximum racemization upon solvolysis under identical conditions?
questions.solution:
Solvolysis reactions proceed via an $S_{N}1$ mechanism, which involves the formation of a carbocation intermediate.

The extent of racemization in an $S_{N}1$ reaction is directly proportional to the stability of the intermediate carbocation. A highly stable carbocation has a longer lifetime, allowing the leaving group to diffuse away completely. The resulting free, planar carbocation can be attacked by a nucleophile from either face with equal probability, leading to maximum racemization.

Evaluating the stability of the carbocations formed by the loss of a chloride ion from the given substrates:

In (A), the benzylic carbocation is highly stabilized by the strong $+M$ (mesomeric) effect of the $-OCH_3$ group at the para position.

In (B), the benzylic carbocation is stabilized by resonance with the unsubstituted benzene ring, but lacks the additional $+M$ stabilization present in (A).

In (C), the benzylic carbocation is strongly destabilized by the electron-withdrawing $-M$ and $-I$ effects of the $-NO_2$ group.

In (D), the cleavage of the $C-Cl$ bond yields a primary aliphatic carbocation ($CH_3-C^{+}HD$), which is highly unstable compared to benzylic carbocations.

Since the carbocation formed from substrate (A) is the most stable, it will have the longest lifetime and thus undergo maximum racemization.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the given $\mathrm{E}_2$ reaction:
questions.solution:
The given substrate is 1-chloro-1-methylcyclopentane, which is a tertiary alkyl halide.

The reagent, sodium ethoxide ($\text{Na}^+\text{OCH}_2\text{CH}_3^-$), is a strong and unhindered base. The reaction of a tertiary alkyl halide with a strong base proceeds predominantly via the $\text{E}_2$ elimination mechanism.

An $\text{E}_2$ elimination requires an anti-periplanar geometry between the leaving group ($\text{Cl}$) and the $\beta$-hydrogen being removed.

In the given reactant, the chlorine atom is positioned on a wedge. The adjacent ring carbons (C2 and C5) each possess two $\beta$-hydrogens: one on a wedge and one on a dash. The $\beta$-hydrogens on the dash are anti-periplanar to the chlorine atom, allowing for elimination to occur within the ring.

Elimination of a ring $\beta$-hydrogen produces 1-methylcyclopentene, which is a trisubstituted alkene.

Alternatively, the methyl group also contains $\beta$-hydrogens. Elimination of a hydrogen from the methyl group produces methylenecyclopentane, which is a disubstituted alkene.

According to Zaitsev's rule, elimination reactions using small, unhindered bases like ethoxide favor the formation of the more substituted and thermodynamically more stable alkene as the major product.

Therefore, the trisubstituted alkene, 1-methylcyclopentene, is the major product.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the product(s) for the given $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction.



(i)
(ii)
(iii)
(iv)
questions.solution:
The given reaction is specified to proceed via an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ mechanism.

In an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction, the first step involves the departure of the leaving group ($\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$) to form a carbocation intermediate. The resulting cyclopentyl carbocation is $sp^2$ hybridized, making it planar at the positively charged carbon atom.

Because the carbocation is planar, the incoming nucleophile ($\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{S}^{-}$) can attack from either face (top or bottom) with equal probability.

Attack from the same face as the original leaving group results in retention of configuration (represented by the dashed wedge, structure i). Attack from the opposite face results in inversion of configuration (represented by the solid wedge, structure ii).

Although the resulting molecule (ethyl cyclopentyl sulfide) is achiral and structures (i) and (ii) are identical (superimposable), they represent the two possible stereochemical outcomes of the nucleophilic attack on the planar intermediate. Therefore, both (i) and (ii) are considered the products of this $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ process.

Structures (iii) and (iv) contain double bonds, which would require an unsaturated starting material or multiple elimination steps, neither of which applies here.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify compound $\mathrm{B}$ in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
When 1,2-dibromocyclopentane reacts with $1$ equivalent of magnesium (Mg) in the presence of dry ether, magnesium inserts into one of the carbon-bromine bonds. This results in the formation of a mono-Grignard reagent.

Thus, compound A is 2-bromocyclopentylmagnesium bromide.

Grignard reagents formed from 1,2-dihalides are generally unstable at higher temperatures. Upon heating ($\Delta$), compound A undergoes an intramolecular $\beta$-elimination reaction. The $-MgBr$ group and the adjacent $-Br$ atom are eliminated as $MgBr_2$, leading to the formation of a double bond between the two carbon atoms.

Therefore, compound B is cyclopentene.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds will yield the Zaitsev (Saytzeff) alkene as the major product in an $\mathrm{E}2$ elimination reaction with an unhindered base (such as sodium ethoxide)?
questions.solution:
In an $\mathrm{E}2$ elimination reaction, the regioselectivity (Zaitsev vs. Hofmann product) depends significantly on the nature of the leaving group and the steric bulk of the base.

With an unhindered base like sodium ethoxide ($\mathrm{EtO^-}$), the major product is determined by the leaving group:
1. Good leaving groups (such as $-\mathrm{Br}$, $-\mathrm{Cl}$, $-\mathrm{I}$) result in a transition state with significant double-bond character. This leads to the formation of the more substituted, more stable alkene as the major product, following Zaitsev's rule.
2. Poor leaving groups (such as $-\mathrm{F}$, $-\mathrm{N^+R_3}$, $-\mathrm{OCOCH_3}$) result in a transition state with significant carbanion character. Since carbanions are more stable on less substituted carbons, the proton is preferentially removed from the less hindered $\beta$-carbon, yielding the less substituted alkene as the major product, following Hofmann's rule.

Evaluating the given options:
- (A) Acetate ($-\mathrm{OCOCH_3}$) is a poor leaving group and typically yields the Hofmann product.
- (B) The quaternary ammonium group ($-\mathrm{N^+Me_3}$) is a poor leaving group and classically undergoes Hofmann elimination.
- (C) Bromide ($-\mathrm{Br}$) is a good leaving group. 2-bromobutane will undergo $\mathrm{E}2$ elimination with sodium ethoxide to yield 2-butene (the more substituted Zaitsev alkene) as the major product.
- (D) Fluoride ($-\mathrm{F}$) is a poor leaving group, so 2-fluorobutane yields 1-butene (the Hofmann alkene) as the major product.

Therefore, 2-bromobutane will yield the Zaitsev alkene as the major product.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the product $\mathrm{X}$ in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reactant is tert-butylmagnesium bromide, which is a Grignard reagent ($R-MgX$).

Grignard reagents are strong bases and react readily with compounds containing acidic hydrogen or deuterium, such as $H_2O$ or $D_2O$, to form the corresponding alkane or deuterated alkane.

In this reaction, the nucleophilic carbanion from the Grignard reagent abstracts a deuterium ion ($D^+$) from heavy water ($D_2O$):

$(CH_3)_3C-MgBr + D_2O \rightarrow (CH_3)_3C-D + Mg(OD)Br$

Thus, the product X is 2-deuterio-2-methylpropane, $(CH_3)_3C-D$.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For which of the following leaving groups, $\mathrm{X}$, will the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction shown below proceed most rapidly?
questions.solution:
The given reaction is an $\text{S}_{\text{N}}2$ substitution where $\text{SH}^{-}$ acts as a nucleophile and $\text{X}^{-}$ acts as a leaving group.

The rate of an $\text{S}_{\text{N}}2$ reaction increases with the leaving group ability of $\text{X}$. A good leaving group is a weak conjugate base.

The conjugate acids of the given leaving groups are $\text{HBr}$, $\text{HI}$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, and $\text{HCl}$.

The acidic strength order of these conjugate acids is:
$\text{HI} > \text{HBr} > \text{HCl} > \text{H}_2\text{O}$

Therefore, the basic strength order of the corresponding anions is:
$\text{I}^{-} < \text{Br}^{-} < \text{Cl}^{-} < \text{OH}^{-}$

Since $\text{I}^{-}$ is the weakest base, it is the most stable anion and thus the best leaving group among the given options. Consequently, the reaction will proceed most rapidly when $\text{X} = -\text{I}$.

Answer: $\mathrm{X} = -\mathrm{I}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the major product of the given reaction?
questions.solution:
Aqueous $\text{AgNO}_3$ abstracts the $\text{Br}^-$ ion to form a primary carbocation adjacent to the cyclopentane ring.

The unstable primary carbocation undergoes a semi-pinacol rearrangement. A ring expansion occurs where a C-C bond of the five-membered ring migrates to the $-\text{CH}_2^+$ carbon, forming a six-membered ring.

The positive charge shifts to the carbon bearing the $-\text{OH}$ group, resulting in a resonance-stabilized oxocarbenium ion.

Loss of a proton from the oxocarbenium ion gives cyclohexanone as the final major product.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following statements are correct for the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction of alkyl halides?
I. Both the alkyl halide and the nucleophile are involved in the transition state.
II. The reaction proceeds with inversion of configuration at the substitution centre.
III. The reaction proceeds with retention of configuration at the substitution centre.
IV. The order of reactivity of alkyl halides is $3^{\circ} > 2^{\circ} > 1^{\circ}$.
V. The nucleophile must have an unshared electron pair and bear a negative charge.
VI. The greater the nucleophilicity of the nucleophile, the greater the rate of the reaction.
questions.solution:
Statement I is correct because the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ mechanism is a concerted single-step process where both the alkyl halide and the nucleophile are present in the transition state.

Statement II is correct because the nucleophile attacks from the side opposite to the leaving group, resulting in the inversion of configuration at the substitution centre.

Statement III is incorrect as $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ proceeds with inversion, not retention.

Statement IV is incorrect because the reactivity order for $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ is $1^{\circ} > 2^{\circ} > 3^{\circ}$ due to steric hindrance.

Statement V is incorrect because a nucleophile can be neutral (e.g., $\mathrm{NH}_3$, $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$) and does not necessarily need to bear a negative charge.

Statement VI is correct because the rate of an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction is directly proportional to the nucleophilicity of the attacking species.

Thus, statements I, II, and VI are correct.

Answer: I, II, and VI

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following chlorides:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
(iv)

The correct order of reactivity of these compounds towards hydrolysis by an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ mechanism is:
questions.solution:
The reactivity of a compound towards an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ mechanism depends on the stability of the carbocation intermediate formed in the rate-determining step.

The carbocations formed by the given chlorides are:
(i) Benzyl cation: $\mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_5\mathrm{CH}_2^+$
(ii) p-Methylbenzyl cation: $p\text{-CH}_3\text{-C}_6\mathrm{H}_4\mathrm{CH}_2^+$
(iii) p-Methoxybenzyl cation: $p\text{-CH}_3\mathrm{O}\text{-C}_6\mathrm{H}_4\mathrm{CH}_2^+$
(iv) p-Nitrobenzyl cation: $p\text{-NO}_2\text{-C}_6\mathrm{H}_4\mathrm{CH}_2^+$

Electron-donating groups stabilize the carbocation, while electron-withdrawing groups destabilize it.

In (iii), the $-\mathrm{OCH}_3$ group exerts a strong $+\mathrm{R}$ (resonance) effect, highly stabilizing the carbocation.
In (ii), the $-\mathrm{CH}_3$ group exerts $+\mathrm{I}$ (inductive) and hyperconjugation effects, stabilizing the carbocation but to a lesser extent than $-\mathrm{OCH}_3$.
In (i), there is no substituent on the benzene ring.
In (iv), the $-\mathrm{NO}_2$ group exerts strong $-\mathrm{R}$ and $-\mathrm{I}$ effects, highly destabilizing the carbocation.

Therefore, the order of stability of the carbocations is (iv) < (i) < (ii) < (iii).

The reactivity towards $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ hydrolysis follows the same order: (iv) < (i) < (ii) < (iii).

Answer: (iv) < (i) < (ii) < (iii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of reactivity of the following compounds towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
The rate of an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction depends on the stability of the intermediate carbocation and the leaving group ability.

In compounds (i) and (ii), the halogen is attached to the carbon adjacent to the oxygen atom (C2 position). Upon departure of the halide ion, the resulting carbocation is adjacent to the oxygen atom. This carbocation is highly stabilized by the $+M$ effect (resonance) of the oxygen lone pair, forming a stable oxocarbenium ion.

In compound (iii), the chlorine atom is attached to the C3 position. The carbocation formed is a secondary carbocation that lacks resonance stabilization and is destabilized by the $-I$ effect of the more electronegative oxygen atom. Thus, compounds (i) and (ii) are much more reactive than (iii).

Between compounds (i) and (ii), the leaving group ability follows the order $\mathrm{I}^{-} > \mathrm{Br}^{-}$ because $\mathrm{I}^{-}$ is a weaker base and a larger, more polarizable ion. Therefore, compound (i) reacts faster than compound (ii).

The overall order of reactivity towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction is (i) > (ii) > (iii).

Answer: (i) > (ii) > (iii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of reactivity of the following compounds towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction.

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
The reactivity of alkyl halides towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction depends on the stability of the carbocation intermediate formed after the departure of the leaving group (chloride ion).

The carbocations formed from the given compounds are:
(I) Cyclopropylmethyl cation
(II) Tricyclopropylmethyl cation
(III) Dicyclopropylmethyl cation

The cyclopropylmethyl cation is exceptionally stable due to the delocalization of the $\sigma$-electrons of the bent bonds (banana bonds) of the cyclopropyl ring into the vacant p-orbital of the adjacent positively charged carbon atom. This phenomenon is often referred to as "dancing resonance".

The stability of the carbocation increases with an increase in the number of cyclopropyl groups attached to the positively charged carbon atom, as more cyclopropyl rings provide greater $\sigma$-conjugation.

Therefore, the order of stability of the carbocations is:
Tricyclopropylmethyl cation > Dicyclopropylmethyl cation > Cyclopropylmethyl cation

Consequently, the correct order of reactivity towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction is (II) > (III) > (I).

Answer: (II) > (III) > (I)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major intermediate involved in the following transformation:
questions.solution:
The transformation of bromobenzene to aniline using a strong base like $\text{KNH}_2$ in liquid $\text{NH}_3$ proceeds via an elimination-addition mechanism.

Since there are no strong electron-withdrawing groups on the benzene ring, the reaction does not follow the usual addition-elimination ($S_NAr$) pathway. Instead, the strong base $\text{NH}_2^-$ abstracts an acidic ortho-proton from bromobenzene, leading to the elimination of the bromide ion.

This elimination step generates a highly reactive neutral intermediate known as benzyne, which contains a formal triple bond in the aromatic ring.

The benzyne intermediate then undergoes nucleophilic attack by $\text{NH}_2^-$ followed by protonation to yield aniline.

Therefore, the major intermediate involved in this reaction is benzyne.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of reactivity for the following compounds towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction.

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
The reactivity of alkyl halides towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction depends on the stability of the intermediate carbocation formed.

Compound (III) forms a secondary cyclohexyl carbocation. This carbocation can achieve the required planar $sp^2$ geometry and is relatively stable.

Compounds (I) and (II) form carbocations at the bridgehead position. According to Bredt's rule, bridgehead carbocations are highly unstable because the rigid bicyclic framework prevents the carbon atom from achieving a planar $sp^2$ hybridization, leading to severe angle strain.

Between the two bridgehead halides, the bicyclo[2.2.2]octane system in (II) is larger and more flexible than the bicyclo[2.2.1]heptane system in (I). This increased flexibility allows the bridgehead carbon in (II) to flatten out slightly more than in (I), making the carbocation of (II) less strained and relatively more stable than that of (I).

Therefore, the order of carbocation stability, and hence the reactivity towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction, is (III) > (II) > (I).

Answer: (III) > (II) > (I)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of reactivity for the following compounds towards an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
The reactivity of a compound towards an $\text{S}_{\text{N}}1$ reaction depends on the stability of the intermediate carbocation formed after the departure of the leaving group (chloride ion).

In compound (i), the departure of the chloride ion forms a carbocation that is highly stabilized by the para-cyclopropyl group. The cyclopropyl ring adopts a bisected conformation, allowing for strong $\sigma-\pi$ conjugation (overlap of Walsh orbitals with the empty p-orbital of the carbocation). This provides exceptional stability, making it the most reactive.

In compound (iii), the carbocation is stabilized by the para-cyclopentyl group through hyperconjugation ($+\text{H}$) and the inductive effect ($+\text{I}$). While stabilizing, this effect is significantly weaker than the $\sigma-\pi$ conjugation provided by the cyclopropyl group in (i). Therefore, (i) is more reactive than (iii).

In compound (ii), the cyclopropyl group is flanked by two bulky methyl groups at its ortho positions (meta to the carbocation center). These methyl groups cause severe steric hindrance, forcing the cyclopropyl ring to rotate out of its bisected conformation. This phenomenon is known as Steric Inhibition of Resonance (SIR). As a result, the $\sigma-\pi$ conjugation is effectively destroyed, and the cyclopropyl group can no longer stabilize the carbocation through resonance. The stabilization is reduced to only the weak inductive effects of the meta-methyl groups and the out-of-plane cyclopropyl group, making this carbocation the least stable among the three.

Thus, the correct order of stability of the intermediate carbocations, and consequently the reactivity towards the $\text{S}_{\text{N}}1$ reaction, is (i) > (iii) > (ii).

Answer: (i) > (iii) > (ii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of reactivity of the following compounds towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
The reactivity of a compound towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction depends on the stability of the intermediate carbocation formed after the departure of the leaving group (tosylate, $-\mathrm{OTs}$).

For compound (i), the departure of $-\mathrm{OTs}$ yields the 1,2,3-tricyclopropylcyclopropenyl cation. This carbocation is highly stable because it is an aromatic system ($2\pi$ electrons) and is further stabilized by the strong $\sigma$-conjugation (dancing resonance) from the three attached cyclopropyl groups.

For compound (ii), the departure of $-\mathrm{OTs}$ yields the cyclopropenyl cation. This cation is also aromatic ($2\pi$ electrons) and thus stable, but it lacks the additional stabilization provided by the cyclopropyl groups present in (i).

For compound (iii), the departure of $-\mathrm{OTs}$ yields the cyclopropyl cation. This cation is highly unstable due to severe angle strain and the absence of any resonance or aromatic stabilization.

Therefore, the order of stability of the intermediate carbocations is (i) > (ii) > (iii).

Consequently, the correct order of reactivity towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction is (i) > (ii) > (iii).

Answer: (i) > (ii) > (iii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of reactivity for the following compounds towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction:

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
The reactivity of alkyl halides towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction depends on the stability of the intermediate carbocation formed.

The given compounds form the following carbocations upon the loss of a chloride ion:
(I) Cyclopropyl cation
(II) Cyclobutyl cation
(III) Cyclopentyl cation

The positively charged carbon atom in a carbocation is $sp^2$ hybridized, which has an ideal bond angle of $120^\circ$. The stability of these cyclic carbocations is determined by the angle strain, which is the difference between the ideal bond angle and the actual internal angle of the ring.

In the cyclopropyl cation, the internal angle is $60^\circ$, leading to severe angle strain.
In the cyclobutyl cation, the internal angle is approximately $90^\circ$, leading to less angle strain than in the cyclopropyl cation.
In the cyclopentyl cation, the internal angle is approximately $108^\circ$, which is closest to the ideal $120^\circ$, resulting in the least angle strain among the three.

As the angle strain decreases, the stability of the carbocation increases. Therefore, the order of stability of the carbocations is:
Cyclopentyl cation > Cyclobutyl cation > Cyclopropyl cation

Consequently, the correct order of reactivity towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction is (III) > (II) > (I).

Answer: (III) > (II) > (I)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct reactivity order for the $\mathrm{S_N1}$ solvolysis reaction of the following compounds:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
The rate of $S_N1$ solvolysis depends on the stability of the carbocation intermediate formed after the leaving group (OTs) departs.

Compound (i) forms a secondary carbocation at the C7 position of the bicyclo[2.2.1]heptane system. This carbocation is highly strained due to the rigid bicyclic framework preventing the preferred planar $sp^2$ geometry, making it unstable.

Compound (ii) forms a carbocation at C7. The adjacent double bond at the C2-C3 position provides significant anchimeric assistance (neighboring group participation) through interaction of the $\pi$-electrons with the developing empty p-orbital. This leads to a resonance-stabilized non-classical carbocation (a bishomocyclopropenyl cation). This substantial stabilization makes compound (ii) extremely reactive towards $S_N1$ solvolysis, much faster than compound (i).

Compound (iii) also has a double bond, but it is located such that the leaving group is 'anti' to it (though technically in this bicyclic system it's about the orbital overlap). In this geometry, the $\pi$-electron cloud is pointing away from the empty p-orbital developing at C7, offering no anchimeric assistance. Furthermore, the inductive electron-withdrawing effect (-I effect) of the $sp^2$ carbons destabilizes the carbocation relative to the saturated analog (i). Therefore, compound (iii) is the least reactive.

Comparing the stabilities of the intermediates, the carbocation from (ii) is highly stabilized by $\pi$-participation, the carbocation from (i) has no such stabilization but only regular alkyl groups, and the carbocation from (iii) is destabilized by the inductive effect of the isolated double bond.

Thus, the reactivity order is (ii) > (i) > (iii).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of reactivity of the following compounds towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction:

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
The reactivity of a compound towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction depends on the stability of the intermediate carbocation formed after the departure of the leaving group.

Let us analyze the carbocations formed from the given compounds:

For compound (I), the departure of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ yields a primary allylic carbocation: $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}^{+}$. This carbocation is stabilized by resonance.

For compound (II), the departure of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ yields a secondary allylic carbocation: $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}^{+}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$. This carbocation is also stabilized by resonance, and additionally, it is stabilized by the $+\mathrm{I}$ effect and hyperconjugation of the methyl group. Hence, it is more stable than the primary allylic carbocation of (I).

For compound (III), the departure of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ yields a vinylic carbocation: $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}^{+}$. This carbocation is highly unstable because the positive charge resides on an electronegative $sp$-hybridized carbon atom. Furthermore, the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond in vinyl chloride has partial double bond character due to resonance, making it difficult to cleave.

Comparing the stabilities of these carbocations, the order is:
Secondary allylic (II) $>$ Primary allylic (I) $>$ Vinylic (III)

Therefore, the correct order of reactivity towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction is $\text{II} > \text{I} > \text{III}$.

Answer: $\text{II} > \text{I} > \text{III}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of reactivity for the following compounds towards an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
The rate of an $S_N1$ reaction depends on the stability of the intermediate carbocation and the relief of steric strain upon departure of the leaving group.

Isomers (i) and (iii) have their methyl groups cis to each other. After the departure of the tosylate leaving group, they form the same cis-3,5-dimethylcyclohexyl carbocation. In this carbocation, both methyl groups can adopt relatively stable pseudo-equatorial positions. Isomer (ii) has its methyl groups trans to each other and forms a trans-3,5-dimethylcyclohexyl carbocation. In this intermediate, one methyl group must occupy a higher-energy pseudo-axial position. Because it forms a less stable carbocation, isomer (ii) has the highest activation energy and is therefore the slowest to react.

To distinguish between the reaction rates of (i) and (iii), we evaluate their ground state conformational stabilities. Isomer (i) is an all-cis isomer. Its most stable chair conformation places all three substituents in equatorial positions, resulting in a very stable, low-energy ground state. Isomer (iii) is a trans,trans isomer. To minimize steric strain, its most stable chair conformation places the two methyl groups in equatorial positions, forcing the tosylate leaving group into an axial position.

The ground state of (iii) is higher in energy than the all-equatorial ground state of (i) due to 1,3-diaxial interactions involving the axial tosylate group. Because isomer (iii) starts from a higher energy ground state, the activation energy required to reach the common carbocation intermediate is smaller. Additionally, an axial leaving group is stereoelectronically favoured to depart faster than an equatorial one. Therefore, (iii) reacts faster than (i).

The correct order of reactivity towards the $S_N1$ reaction is (iii) > (i) > (ii).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of reactivity of the following compounds towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction.

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
The reactivity of alkyl halides towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction depends on the stability of the carbocation intermediate formed in the rate-determining step.

Let us analyze the carbocations formed by the heterolytic cleavage of the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Br}$ bond in the given compounds:

For compound (I), the departure of the bromide ion forms a secondary ($2^\circ$) carbocation (cyclohexyl cation). The positively charged carbon is adjacent to two $-\mathrm{CH}_2-$ groups, providing $2 + 2 = 4$ $\alpha$-hydrogens for stabilization via hyperconjugation.

For compound (II), the departure of the bromide ion forms a tertiary ($3^\circ$) carbocation (1-methylcyclohexyl cation). The positively charged carbon is adjacent to one $-\mathrm{CH}_3$ group and two $-\mathrm{CH}_2-$ groups, providing $3 + 2 + 2 = 7$ $\alpha$-hydrogens.

For compound (III), the departure of the bromide ion forms a secondary ($2^\circ$) carbocation (2-methylcyclohexyl cation). The positively charged carbon is adjacent to one $-\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{CH}_3)-$ group and one $-\mathrm{CH}_2-$ group, providing $1 + 2 = 3$ $\alpha$-hydrogens.

The stability of a carbocation increases with the number of $\alpha$-hydrogens due to hyperconjugation. Furthermore, tertiary carbocations are inherently more stable than secondary carbocations.

Comparing the number of $\alpha$-hydrogens, the stability order of the intermediate carbocations is:
(II) ($7$ $\alpha$-H) > (I) ($4$ $\alpha$-H) > (III) ($3$ $\alpha$-H)

Since a more stable carbocation forms faster, the correct order of reactivity towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction is (II) > (I) > (III).

Answer: (II) > (I) > (III)

questions.q:
Identify the correct order of reactivity of the following compounds towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction:

questions.solution:
The reactivity of a compound towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction depends on the stability of the intermediate carbocation formed after the departure of the leaving group (chloride ion).

In compound (i), the departure of the chloride ion generates a secondary benzylic carbocation, which is stabilized by resonance with the adjacent benzene ring.

In compound (ii), the carbocation formed is adjacent to both the benzene ring and an oxygen atom. It is stabilized by resonance with the benzene ring as well as the strong $+\mathrm{M}$ effect of the lone pairs on the oxygen atom.

In compound (iii), the carbocation formed is adjacent to both the benzene ring and an $\mathrm{NH}$ group. It is stabilized by resonance with the benzene ring and the strong $+\mathrm{M}$ effect of the lone pair on the nitrogen atom.

Since nitrogen is less electronegative than oxygen, it donates its lone pair more readily than oxygen. Therefore, the $+\mathrm{M}$ effect of the $-\mathrm{NH}-$ group is stronger than that of the $-\mathrm{O}-$ atom, making the carbocation from (iii) more stable than that from (ii). Both heteroatom-stabilized carbocations are significantly more stable than the simple benzylic carbocation from (i).

Thus, the order of stability of the intermediate carbocations is (iii) $>$ (ii) $>$ (i).

Consequently, the correct order of reactivity towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction is (iii) $>$ (ii) $>$ (i).

Answer: (iii) > (ii) > (i)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of reactivity of the following compounds towards the $\mathrm{S_N1}$ reaction:

(i)

questions.solution:
The reactivity of alkyl halides towards the $\mathrm{S_N1}$ reaction depends directly on the stability of the carbocation intermediate formed after the departure of the leaving group (halide ion).

Let us analyze the carbocations formed from the given compounds:

1. Compound (i) is cyclopropylmethyl chloride. Upon losing the chloride ion, it forms the cyclopropylmethyl carbocation. This carbocation is exceptionally stable due to the delocalization of the positive charge with the $\sigma$-electrons of the adjacent cyclopropane ring (often referred to as dancing resonance).

2. Compound (ii) is 2-chloropropene. The loss of the chloride ion yields the 2-propenyl carbocation ($\mathrm{CH_3-\overset{+}{C}=CH_2}$). This is a vinylic carbocation, which is generally unstable because the positive charge resides on an more electronegative sp-hybridized carbon. However, the presence of the electron-donating methyl group provides some stabilization through the +I effect.

3. Compound (iii) is vinyl chloride. It forms the vinyl carbocation ($\mathrm{CH_2=\overset{+}{C}H}$). This is also a vinylic carbocation but lacks any alkyl group to stabilize the positive charge, making it highly unstable and the least stable among the three.

Comparing the stabilities of these carbocations, the cyclopropylmethyl carbocation is by far the most stable. Between the two vinylic carbocations, the one with the methyl group is more stable than the unsubstituted one.

Therefore, the order of stability of the carbocations is: (i) > (ii) > (iii).

Consequently, the correct order of reactivity towards the $\mathrm{S_N1}$ reaction is (i) > (ii) > (iii).

Answer: (i) > (ii) > (iii)

questions.solution:
The reactivity of alkyl halides towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction depends on the steric hindrance around the electrophilic carbon atom. A less sterically hindered carbon allows the nucleophile to attack more easily from the backside.

Let us identify the degree of the given alkyl halides:
(I) tert-butyl chloride is a tertiary ($3^{\circ}$) alkyl halide.
(II) isopropyl chloride is a secondary ($2^{\circ}$) alkyl halide.
(III) ethyl chloride is a primary ($1^{\circ}$) alkyl halide.

The steric hindrance increases in the order: $1^{\circ} < 2^{\circ} < 3^{\circ}$.
Therefore, the reactivity towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction follows the reverse order: $1^{\circ} > 2^{\circ} > 3^{\circ}$.

Thus, the correct decreasing order of reactivity is (III) > (II) > (I).

Answer: (III) > (II) > (I)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of reactivity of the following compounds towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction:

(I)

(II)

(III)
questions.solution:
The reactivity of a compound towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction depends on the stability of the carbocation intermediate formed after the departure of the leaving group ($\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$).

In compound (I), the loss of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ generates a carbocation at the C4 position. The oxygen atom donates its lone pair of electrons to the conjugated system, resulting in a cyclic, planar structure with $6\pi$ electrons. This forms the pyrylium ion, which is aromatic and highly stable.

In compound (II), the loss of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ generates a carbocation that is stabilized by the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom. This forms the pyridinium ion, which also has $6\pi$ electrons and is aromatic.

In compound (III), the loss of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ generates a carbocation in a ring containing a boron atom. The boron atom has an empty p-orbital and contributes zero $\pi$ electrons. The resulting cyclic, planar system has only $4\pi$ electrons from the two double bonds. This makes the carbocation antiaromatic and highly unstable.

Comparing the aromatic carbocations formed from (I) and (II), nitrogen is less electronegative than oxygen. As a result, nitrogen can more easily donate its lone pair and better accommodate the positive charge compared to oxygen. Therefore, the pyridinium ion is more stable than the pyrylium ion.

The order of stability of the intermediate carbocations is (II) > (I) > (III). Consequently, the correct order of reactivity towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction is (II) > (I) > (III).

Answer: (II) > (I) > (III)

Identify the correct order of reactivity for the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction of the following compounds:

(i)

(iii)
questions.solution:
The rate of an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction depends on the stability of the carbocation formed and the leaving group ability.

Since the alkyl group is the same (tert-butyl) in all three compounds, the intermediate formed is the tert-butyl carbocation in each case. Thus, the reactivity is determined solely by the leaving group ability.

A better leaving group is a weaker base, which corresponds to a stronger conjugate acid. The leaving groups in the given compounds are:
(i) Triflate ion ($\mathrm{CF}_{3}\mathrm{SO}_{3}^{-}$)
(ii) Chloride ion ($\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$)
(iii) Brosylate ion ($p\text{-}\mathrm{BrC}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{4}\mathrm{SO}_{3}^{-}$)

The triflate ion is an exceptionally good leaving group because the strong electron-withdrawing $-\mathrm{CF}_{3}$ group highly stabilizes the negative charge on the sulfonate oxygen atoms through the inductive effect, in addition to resonance. It is one of the best leaving groups known.

The brosylate ion is also a very good leaving group (better than halides) because its negative charge is delocalized over three oxygen atoms via resonance, and the $-\mathrm{Br}$ atom provides a mild electron-withdrawing effect.

The chloride ion is a good leaving group but significantly less effective than the sulfonate leaving groups (triflate and brosylate).

Therefore, the order of leaving group ability is: Triflate > Brosylate > Chloride.

This gives the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reactivity order as (i) > (iii) > (ii).

Answer: (i) > (iii) > (ii)

Identify the correct decreasing order of reactivity of the following compounds towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction:

(III)
questions.solution:
The reactivity of a compound towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction depends on the stability of the intermediate carbocation formed after the departure of the leaving group ($\mathrm{Br}^{-}$).

Let us analyze the carbocations formed from each compound:

1. Compound (I) forms the benzyl carbocation ($\mathrm{Ph}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}^{+}$). This carbocation is stabilized by resonance with the $\pi$-electrons of the benzene ring.

2. Compound (II) forms the phenyl carbocation ($\mathrm{Ph}^{+}$). This carbocation is highly unstable because the positive charge resides on an $sp^2$ hybridized carbon atom and cannot be stabilized by resonance.

3. Compound (III) forms the cyclopropylmethyl carbocation. This carbocation is exceptionally stable due to the delocalization of the $\sigma$-electrons of the cyclopropane ring into the empty p-orbital of the positively charged carbon (a phenomenon known as dancing resonance or $\sigma$-conjugation). The stabilization provided by this effect is even greater than the resonance stabilization in the benzyl carbocation.

Therefore, the order of stability of the intermediate carbocations is:
Cyclopropylmethyl carbocation > Benzyl carbocation > Phenyl carbocation

Consequently, the decreasing order of reactivity towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction is (III) > (I) > (II).

Answer: (III) > (I) > (II)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Determine the correct order of reactivity of the following compounds towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction:

(III)
questions.solution:
The rate of an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction depends on the stability of the intermediate carbocation formed after the departure of the leaving group ($\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$).

Let us analyze the intermediate carbocations formed for each compound:

1. For compound (I), the departure of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ generates a carbocation at C-2, which is flanked by two $-\mathrm{NH}-$ groups. The nitrogen atoms have lone pairs that strongly stabilize the positive charge through resonance ($+\mathrm{M}$ effect). Since nitrogen is less electronegative than oxygen, it is a better electron donor, making this carbocation highly stable (similar to an amidinium ion).

2. For compound (II), the carbocation is flanked by two $-\mathrm{O}-$ atoms. The oxygen atoms also stabilize the positive charge through resonance ($+\mathrm{M}$ effect) by completing the octet of the carbon atom. However, because oxygen is more electronegative than nitrogen, its electron-donating tendency is weaker than that of nitrogen. Thus, this carbocation is less stable than that of (I) but still highly stabilized by resonance.

3. For compound (III), the departure of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ forms a secondary cyclohexyl cation. This carbocation is stabilized only by hyperconjugation ($4\alpha$-hydrogens) and the $+\mathrm{I}$ effect of the alkyl chain. It lacks the strong resonance stabilization provided by heteroatoms with lone pairs, making it the least stable among the three.

Therefore, the stability order of the intermediate carbocations is (I) > (II) > (III).

Consequently, the correct order of reactivity towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction is (I) > (II) > (III).

Answer: (I) > (II) > (III)

The formation of a carbocation at this position is highly unfavorable because the rigid bicyclic structure prevents the carbon from adopting the planar $sp^2$ geometry required for a stable carbocation (Bredt's rule). Thus, compound (I) is the least reactive.

In compound (II), the departure of the bromide ion generates a tertiary carbocation, as the carbon is bonded to three other carbons (including the methyl group). Tertiary carbocations are highly stable due to inductive effects and hyperconjugation.

In compound (III), the departure of the bromide ion generates a secondary carbocation, which is less stable than the tertiary carbocation formed in compound (II).

Therefore, the stability order of the intermediate carbocations is (II) > (III) > (I), which corresponds to their reactivity order towards the $\text{S}_{\text{N}}1$ reaction.

Answer: (II) > (III) > (I)

The rate of an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction is directly proportional to the stability of the carbocation intermediate formed in the rate-determining step.

For compound (i), the leaving group is located at a bridgehead position. The resulting bridgehead carbocation is highly unstable due to Bredt's rule, as the rigid bicyclic framework prevents the carbon from adopting the necessary planar $sp^2$ hybridization. Consequently, compound (i) is the least reactive.

For compound (ii), ionization yields a carbocation flanked by two oxygen atoms. This intermediate is strongly stabilized by the +M (mesomeric) effect from the lone pairs of both oxygen atoms, allowing extensive delocalization of the positive charge.

Since stabilization by two electron-donating oxygen atoms is greater than that by one, the carbocation derived from (ii) is more stable than the one from (iii).

Therefore, the correct reactivity order for the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction is (ii) > (iii) > (i).
