questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of reactivity for the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction of the following compounds:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
The given compounds are derivatives of benzyl chloride undergoing an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction.

In an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction, the rate depends on the electrophilicity of the carbon atom undergoing substitution and the stability of the transition state. The transition state in an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction has a partial negative charge on the central carbon atom.

Electron-withdrawing groups (EWG) such as $-\mathrm{NO}_2$ exert $-I$ and $-R$ effects. They withdraw electron density from the benzene ring, which increases the electrophilicity of the benzylic carbon and stabilizes the negatively charged transition state. This significantly increases the rate of the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction.

Electron-donating groups (EDG) such as $-\mathrm{OCH}_3$ exert a strong $+R$ effect. They donate electron density to the benzene ring, which decreases the electrophilicity of the benzylic carbon and destabilizes the transition state. This decreases the rate of the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction.

Compound (iii) is unsubstituted benzyl chloride, which serves as the reference.

Therefore, the reactivity order for the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction is:
p-nitrobenzyl chloride (i) > benzyl chloride (iii) > p-methoxybenzyl chloride (ii).

This corresponds to the order (i) > (iii) > (ii).

Answer: (i) > (iii) > (ii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct decreasing order of reactivity of the given compounds towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
The rate of an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction is inversely proportional to the steric hindrance around the electrophilic carbon atom. A bulkier group near the reaction center blocks the backside attack of the incoming nucleophile, thereby decreasing the reactivity.

Let us analyze the steric hindrance in the given compounds:

1. In compound (i), 1-chloro-2,2,6,6-tetramethylcyclohexane, the carbon bonded to the chlorine atom is flanked by two quaternary $\beta$-carbons (C2 and C6). The four bulky methyl groups at the $\beta$-positions severely block the backside approach of the nucleophile. This makes it highly sterically hindered and the least reactive among the three.

2. In compound (ii), 1-chloro-3,3-dimethylcyclohexane, the $\beta$-carbons are unsubstituted ($\mathrm{-CH_2-}$ groups), but there is one quaternary $\gamma$-carbon (C3) with two methyl groups. This provides moderate steric hindrance, primarily from the axial methyl group during the nucleophilic attack, but it is significantly less hindered than compound (i).

3. In compound (iii), 1-chloro-3,3,5,5-tetramethylcyclohexane, there are two quaternary $\gamma$-carbons (C3 and C5), each with two methyl groups. The presence of two axial methyl groups at C3 and C5 creates greater steric hindrance for the incoming nucleophile compared to compound (ii), but it is still less hindered than the $\beta$-substituted compound (i).

Therefore, the order of steric hindrance is (i) > (iii) > (ii).

Consequently, the decreasing order of reactivity towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction is the reverse of the steric hindrance order: (ii) > (iii) > (i).

Answer: (ii) > (iii) > (i)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct reactivity order for the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction among the following compounds:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
The reactivity of $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reactions at the $\alpha$-position of heteroatoms depends on the polarizability of the heteroatom, steric factors, and ground-state stabilization.

1. Polarizability: Sulfur is larger and much more polarizable than oxygen. In the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ transition state, the highly polarizable sulfur atom can better stabilize the partial charges and the electron-rich transition state compared to the less polarizable oxygen atom. This significantly lowers the activation energy for the reaction.

2. Anomeric Effect (Ground-State Stabilization): In 2-chloro-1,3-dioxane (i), the lone pairs on the highly electronegative oxygen atoms strongly donate into the $\sigma^{*}$ orbital of the $\mathrm{C-Cl}$ bond (anomeric effect). This strong $2p-2p$ overlap stabilizes the ground state and partially fills the LUMO ($\sigma^{*}$ orbital), making it less susceptible to nucleophilic attack. In 2-chloro-1,3-dithiane (iii), the $3p-2p$ overlap is much weaker, resulting in a weaker anomeric effect. Thus, the ground state is less stabilized, and the LUMO is more available for the incoming nucleophile.

3. Steric Hindrance: The $\mathrm{C-S}$ bond length ($\sim 1.8 \ \text{\AA}$) is longer than the $\mathrm{C-O}$ bond length ($\sim 1.4 \ \text{\AA}$). The longer bonds in the dithiane ring keep the bulky sulfur atoms further away from the electrophilic carbon, reducing steric hindrance for the approaching nucleophile compared to the dioxane ring.

Because of these factors, $\alpha$-chloro sulfides are significantly more reactive towards $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reactions than $\alpha$-chloro ethers.

Therefore, the reactivity increases as the number of sulfur atoms replacing oxygen atoms increases. The correct $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reactivity order is (iii) > (ii) > (i).

Answer: (iii) > (ii) > (i)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of reactivity of the following compounds towards an $\mathrm{E}2$ elimination reaction using alcoholic $\mathrm{KOH}$:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
The given reaction is an $\mathrm{E}2$ elimination, which proceeds via a concerted, single-step mechanism.

In the rate-determining step of an $\mathrm{E}2$ reaction, the base abstracts a $\beta$-hydrogen (or its isotope) simultaneously with the departure of the leaving group. Thus, the breaking of the $\beta$-$\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ (or $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{D}$, $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{T}$) bond is involved in the rate-determining step.

The bond dissociation energy of carbon-isotope bonds follows the order: $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{T} > \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{D} > \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$.

Because the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bond is the weakest among them, it is broken most easily and rapidly by the base. This phenomenon is known as the primary kinetic isotope effect.

Therefore, the rate of $\mathrm{E}2$ elimination follows the order: $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H} > \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{D} > \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{T}$.

This gives the reactivity order: $(\mathrm{CH}_3)_3\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl} > (\mathrm{CD}_3)_3\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl} > (\mathrm{CT}_3)_3\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}$, which corresponds to (i) > (ii) > (iii).

Answer: (i) > (ii) > (iii)

questions.q:
Identify the correct reactivity order for the $\mathrm{E}_{2}$ elimination reaction with alcoholic KOH for the following compounds:
(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
The rate of an $E_2$ elimination reaction is heavily influenced by the stability of the alkene product formed, as the transition state has partial double-bond character. A more stable product lowers the activation energy, leading to a faster reaction.

Let us analyze the product formed by the elimination of HCl for each compound:

For compound (i), the elimination of HCl yields cyclooctatetraene. Cyclooctatetraene is a non-aromatic compound because it adopts a non-planar, tub-like conformation to avoid anti-aromatic destabilization.

For compound (ii), the elimination of HCl yields benzene. Benzene is an aromatic compound and is exceptionally stable. The formation of an aromatic ring provides a very strong thermodynamic driving force, making this elimination reaction the fastest.

For compound (iii), the elimination of HCl yields cyclobutadiene. Cyclobutadiene is an anti-aromatic compound (having $4\pi$ electrons in a planar ring) and is highly unstable. The transition state leading to its formation is strongly destabilized, making this elimination reaction extremely slow.

Comparing the stabilities of the resulting products: Aromatic (benzene) > Non-aromatic (cyclooctatetraene) > Anti-aromatic (cyclobutadiene).

Therefore, the correct reactivity order for the $E_2$ elimination reaction is (ii) > (i) > (iii).

Answer: ii > i > iii

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct reactivity order for the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction of the following substituted cyclohexyl chlorides:

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
In conformationally locked substituted cyclohexanes, the rate of the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction depends on the steric hindrance encountered by the incoming nucleophile and the leaving group during the transition state.

In structure (I), the bulky tert-butyl group locks the ring such that the chlorine atom is in the axial position. The nucleophile attacks from the backside, which is the equatorial direction. This trajectory is relatively unhindered, making the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction fast.

In structure (II), the chlorine atom is in the equatorial position. The backside attack must occur from the axial direction. This trajectory is highly sterically hindered by the two axial hydrogen atoms at the 3- and 5-positions (1,3-diaxial interactions with the incoming nucleophile). Thus, the reaction is very slow. Therefore, the reactivity of (I) > (II).

In structure (III), the chlorine atom is axial, but there is an additional axial methyl group at the 3-position relative to the chlorine (1,3-diaxial relationship). During the transition state, the reacting carbon becomes $sp^2$ hybridized, causing the ring to flatten locally. This flattening tilts the axial leaving group inwards, pushing it closer to the axial methyl group and increasing steric strain in the transition state. This retards the reaction compared to (I), so (I) > (III).

Comparing (III) and (II), the steric hindrance to the incoming nucleophile in (II) is a more severe barrier than the hindrance to the leaving group in (III). Thus, (III) > (II).

The overall reactivity order for the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction is I > III > II.

Answer: I > III > II

2. Compound (iii) is 1-chloro-3,3-dimethylcyclohexane. It has two methyl groups at the 3-position. In its chair conformation, one of these methyl groups must be axial. This axial methyl group provides significant steric hindrance to the incoming nucleophile, making it less reactive than compound (i).

3. Compound (ii) is 1-chloro-3,3,5,5-tetramethylcyclohexane. It has methyl groups at both the 3- and 5-positions. In its chair conformation, there will be an axial methyl group at C3 and another axial methyl group at C5. These two axial methyl groups severely block the backside of C1, making the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ attack extremely difficult. Thus, it has the maximum steric hindrance and is the least reactive.

Therefore, the order of steric hindrance is (ii) > (iii) > (i), which means the order of reactivity towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction is (i) > (iii) > (ii).

Answer: (i) > (iii) > (ii)

In an $\mathrm{E}2$ elimination reaction, the process is concerted, meaning the abstraction of the $\beta$-proton by the base and the departure of the leaving group occur simultaneously in the rate-determining step.

The rate of the reaction depends significantly on the strength of the carbon-hydrogen (or its isotopes) bond being broken. The bond dissociation energy of carbon-isotope bonds follows the order: $\mathrm{C-T} > \mathrm{C-D} > \mathrm{C-H}$.

Identify the correct order of reactivity for the following compounds towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction:

(I)
(II)
(III) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{Cl}$
questions.solution:
The reactivity of alkyl halides towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction depends on the steric hindrance and the stability of the transition state.

In compound (I), 1-chloro-2-propanone (an $\alpha$-haloketone), the adjacent carbonyl group strongly stabilizes the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ transition state through the overlap of its empty $\pi^{*}$ orbital with the partially filled $p$-orbital of the transition state. This makes it exceptionally reactive.

In compound (II), 3-chloro-1-propene (an allylic halide), the adjacent $\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}$ double bond also stabilizes the transition state through $\pi$-conjugation, making it more reactive than a standard primary alkyl halide.

Compound (III), 1-chloroethane, is a simple primary alkyl halide with no such adjacent groups to stabilize the transition state.

The stabilization provided by the carbonyl group in (I) is significantly greater than that provided by the double bond in (II).

Therefore, the correct order of reactivity towards the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction is (I) > (II) > (III).

Answer: (I) > (II) > (III)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct decreasing order of the rate of solvolysis for the following compounds:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
The rate of solvolysis depends on the stability of the carbocation intermediate and the presence of neighboring group participation (anchimeric assistance).

In compound (ii), the cyclopropane ring is anti to the leaving tosylate (-OTs) group. The $\sigma$-electrons of the cyclopropane ring are perfectly aligned to provide strong anchimeric assistance via backside attack as the -OTs group departs. This leads to the formation of a highly stabilized non-classical carbocation, making the rate of solvolysis exceptionally fast.

In compound (iii), the cyclopropane ring is syn to the -OTs group. Because it is on the same side as the leaving group, it cannot provide backside anchimeric assistance. However, the cyclopropane ring can still offer more stabilization to the developing carbocation than a simple saturated alkane bridge due to its higher electron density and polarizability. Thus, it solvolyzes faster than the completely saturated analog.

In compound (i), there is no cyclopropane ring or double bond to provide any neighboring group participation. The resulting 7-norbornyl cation is highly strained and very unstable, resulting in the slowest rate of solvolysis.

Therefore, the decreasing order of the rate of solvolysis is (ii) > (iii) > (i).

Answer: (ii) > (iii) > (i)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of their rate of solvolysis:

(I)

(II)

(III)
questions.solution:
The solvolysis of anti-7-norbornenyl chlorides proceeds via neighboring group participation (NGP) by the $\pi$-electrons of the double bond, leading to the formation of a stabilized non-classical carbocation.

The rate of solvolysis is directly proportional to the electron density of the double bond. A higher electron density increases the nucleophilicity of the $\pi$-bond, which facilitates NGP and stabilizes the intermediate non-classical carbocation.

In compound (III), the two methyl groups attached to the double bond are electron-donating due to their +I effect and hyperconjugation. This significantly increases the electron density of the double bond, making it the most reactive towards solvolysis.

In compound (II), there are no substituents on the double bond, serving as a standard for comparison.

In compound (I), the chlorine atom attached to the double bond is electron-withdrawing because its -I effect dominates over its +M effect. This decreases the electron density of the $\pi$-bond, making NGP less effective and destabilizing the transition state. Thus, it is the least reactive.

Therefore, the decreasing order of the rate of solvolysis is (III) > (II) > (I).

Answer: (III) > (II) > (I)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of reactivity of the following compounds towards $\mathrm{E}2$ elimination with alcoholic $\mathrm{KOH}$:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
The given compounds undergo $\mathrm{E}2$ elimination with alcoholic $\mathrm{KOH}$ to form a double bond at the bridgehead position.

According to Bredt's rule, the formation of a double bond at a bridgehead carbon is highly restricted in small bicyclic systems due to severe angle strain. The stability of a bridgehead alkene increases as the size of the rings in the bicyclic system increases. Larger rings provide greater flexibility, which better accommodates the planar geometry of the double bond and reduces the overall strain.

The given compounds are:
(i) 1-chlorobicyclo[2.2.1]heptane
(ii) 1-chlorobicyclo[2.2.2]octane
(iii) 1-chlorobicyclo[3.2.2]nonane

The size of the bicyclic system and its flexibility increase in the order (i) < (ii) < (iii). Consequently, the strain in the transition state and the resulting bridgehead alkene decreases in the order (i) > (ii) > (iii).

Therefore, the ease of formation of the bridgehead alkene, and hence the reactivity towards $\mathrm{E}2$ elimination, follows the order (iii) > (ii) > (i).

Answer: iii > ii > i

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct decreasing order of the rate of solvolysis for the following compounds:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
The rate of solvolysis depends on the stability of the intermediate carbocation and the stabilization of the transition state during its formation.

In compound (iii), the chlorine atom is at the bridgehead position. Solvolysis would require the formation of a carbocation at the bridgehead carbon. According to Bredt's rule, a bridgehead carbocation in a small bicyclic system is highly unstable because it cannot achieve the required planar $sp^2$ geometry due to severe angle strain. Therefore, compound (iii) will have the slowest rate of solvolysis.

In compound (i), the chlorine atom is at the endo position. The sulfur atom at the 7-position has lone pairs of electrons. Because the sulfur bridge is located on the exo face, it is perfectly positioned antiperiplanar to the endo C-Cl bond. The sulfur atom can provide strong anchimeric assistance (Neighboring Group Participation, NGP) by attacking the C2 carbon from the backside, displacing the chloride ion and forming a relatively stable bridged sulfonium ion intermediate. This NGP greatly accelerates the rate of solvolysis.

In compound (ii), the chlorine atom is at the exo position. The leaving group is on the same side as the sulfur bridge. Consequently, the sulfur atom cannot perform a backside attack on the C-Cl bond, and no NGP by sulfur is possible. Thus, its solvolysis is much slower than that of compound (i).

Therefore, the decreasing order of the rate of solvolysis is (i) > (ii) > (iii).

Answer: (i) > (ii) > (iii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of reactivity of the following compounds towards $\mathrm{E}_2$ elimination with alcoholic $\mathrm{KOH}$.

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
For an $\text{E}_2$ elimination to occur in cyclohexane derivatives, the leaving group ($\text{Cl}$) and the $\beta$-hydrogen must be in an anti-periplanar geometry, which requires both to occupy axial positions in the chair conformation.

Compound (i): cis-1-chloro-2-methylcyclohexane
The substituents are cis ($\text{Cl}$ is down, $\text{Me}$ is down). In the most stable chair conformation, the bulkier methyl group occupies the equatorial position (down), forcing the chlorine atom into the axial position (down). In this major conformation, the $\beta$-hydrogen at C-2 is axial (up) and is perfectly anti-periplanar to the axial $\text{Cl}$. Elimination occurs readily to form the more stable Saytzeff product (1-methylcyclohexene). Thus, it is highly reactive.

Compound (ii): trans-1-chloro-2-methylcyclohexane
The substituents are trans ($\text{Cl}$ is down, $\text{Me}$ is up). The most stable chair conformation has both $\text{Cl}$ and $\text{Me}$ in equatorial positions, from which $\text{E}_2$ elimination cannot occur. The reactive conformation requires both $\text{Cl}$ and $\text{Me}$ to be axial, which is highly unstable due to 1,2-diaxial interactions. Even in this minor conformation, the $\beta$-hydrogen at C-2 is equatorial, so elimination can only occur using the axial hydrogen at C-6, yielding the less stable Hofmann product (3-methylcyclohexene). Thus, it is much less reactive than (i).

Compound (iii): 2-chloro-1,1,3-trimethylcyclohexane
Here, $\text{Cl}$ is down at C-1, $\text{Me}$ is up at C-2, and there are two $\text{Me}$ groups at C-6. For $\text{E}_2$ elimination, $\text{Cl}$ must be axial (down). In this conformation, the adjacent carbons C-2 and C-6 must provide an axial (up) hydrogen. However, at C-2, the "up" position is occupied by a methyl group (axial). At C-6, the "up" position is also occupied by a methyl group (axial). Since there are no axial $\beta$-hydrogens available in the reactive conformation, compound (iii) cannot undergo $\text{E}_2$ elimination and is the least reactive.

Therefore, the correct order of reactivity towards $\text{E}_2$ elimination is $\text{(i)} > \text{(ii)} > \text{(iii)}$.

Answer: $\text{(i)} > \text{(ii)} > \text{(iii)}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct decreasing order of reactivity for the following compounds towards an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ substitution reaction:

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
The reactivity of a compound towards an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction depends on the steric hindrance around the electrophilic carbon and the electronic stabilization of the transition state.

1. Compound (I) - Vinyl chloride: The chlorine atom is attached to an $sp^2$ hybridized carbon. The lone pairs on the chlorine atom are in conjugation with the $\pi$ bond, giving the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond partial double bond character. This makes the bond difficult to break. Furthermore, $sp^2$ carbons are highly unreactive towards $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reactions due to electrostatic repulsion of the incoming nucleophile by the $\pi$ electron cloud. Thus, (I) is the least reactive.

2. Compound (II) - Allyl chloride: The chlorine is attached to an $sp^3$ hybridized carbon adjacent to a carbon-carbon double bond. The $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ transition state is stabilized by the overlap of the adjacent $\pi$ orbital with the partially formed/broken bonds in the transition state. This makes allyl chloride quite reactive towards $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reactions.

3. Compound (III) - Chloroacetonitrile: The chlorine is attached to an $sp^3$ hybridized carbon adjacent to a cyano ($-\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{N}$) group. The $-\mathrm{CN}$ group is a strong electron-withdrawing group ($-I$ and $-M$ effects). It increases the electrophilicity of the $\alpha$-carbon, making it highly susceptible to nucleophilic attack. Additionally, the $\pi^{*}$ orbital of the cyano group strongly stabilizes the electron-rich $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ transition state. This stabilization is significantly greater than that provided by a simple vinyl group, making $\alpha$-halonitriles exceptionally reactive.

Therefore, the decreasing order of reactivity towards $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ substitution is (III) > (II) > (I).

Answer: (III) > (II) > (I)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reactions will proceed at the fastest rate under identical conditions?
questions.solution:
The given reactions are $S_{N}2$ substitutions where different nucleophiles attack butyl methanesulfonate.

The solvent used is ethanol, which is a polar protic solvent. In polar protic solvents, smaller anions are heavily solvated due to strong hydrogen bonding, which decreases their nucleophilicity.

The order of nucleophilicity for halide ions in polar protic solvents is $I^{-} > Br^{-} > Cl^{-} > F^{-}$.

Neutral molecules like ethanol ($CH_{3}CH_{2}OH$) are weaker nucleophiles compared to anionic nucleophiles like $I^{-}$.

Therefore, $I^{-}$ is the strongest nucleophile among the given choices, and the $S_{N}2$ reaction involving $I^{-}$ will proceed at the fastest rate.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The bulky tert-butyl group locks the cyclohexane ring into a chair conformation where it exclusively occupies the equatorial position to minimize steric strain.

Assigning relative positions, let the carbon with the tert-butyl group be C4, the carbon with the carboxylic acid be C2, and the carbon with the bromine be C1. The tert-butyl group is on a wedge, indicating an up direction. For an up substituent to be equatorial at C4, the positions at the other carbons follow the alternating axial/equatorial pattern: C4(up=equatorial), C3(up=axial), C2(up=equatorial), and C1(up=axial).

At C1, the bromine atom is on a wedge (up), placing it in an axial position. At C2, the COOH group is on a dash (down). Since up is equatorial at C2, the down COOH group must occupy an axial position.

Consequently, the bromine atom and the carboxylic acid group are situated on adjacent carbons in a trans-diaxial arrangement, making them strictly anti-periplanar to each other.

Under basic conditions with heat, the carboxylic acid is deprotonated to form a carboxylate anion. Because of the anti-periplanar geometry, the molecule undergoes a concerted decarboxylative elimination. The carboxylate group acts as an internal electron donor, simultaneously expelling carbon dioxide and the bromide leaving group.

This elimination forms a pi bond between C1 and C2, yielding 4-tert-butylcyclohexene as the major product. The resulting structure lacks both the bromine and the carboxyl group, which corresponds to option (1).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following two elimination reactions:

(i)

(ii)

Which of the two reactions proceeds at a faster rate, and what is the predominant mechanism for these eliminations?
questions.solution:
The given reactions involve the elimination of $\mathrm{HCl}$ from alkyl halides using a strong base, ethoxide ion ($\mathrm{EtO}^{-}$), in ethanol. The presence of a strong base favors the $\mathrm{E2}$ (bimolecular elimination) mechanism for both primary and secondary alkyl halides.

In an $\mathrm{E2}$ reaction, the rate of elimination depends on the stability of the transition state, which has partial double bond character. The more substituted the developing double bond, the more stable the transition state, and the lower the activation energy.

Reaction (i) involves a primary alkyl halide (1-chloropentane) and forms a monosubstituted alkene (1-pentene).

Reaction (ii) involves a secondary alkyl halide (3-chloropentane) and forms a disubstituted alkene (2-pentene).

Since 2-pentene is more stable than 1-pentene, the transition state for Reaction (ii) is more stable than that for Reaction (i). Consequently, the general order of reactivity of alkyl halides towards $\mathrm{E2}$ elimination is $3^{\circ} > 2^{\circ} > 1^{\circ}$.

Therefore, Reaction (ii) proceeds at a faster rate, and the predominant mechanism is $\mathrm{E2}$.

Answer: Reaction (ii), $\mathrm{E}2$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct decreasing order of the rate of solvolysis for the following compounds:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
The rate of solvolysis via the $S_N1$ mechanism depends on the stability of the intermediate carbocation formed after the departure of the leaving group (tosylate, OTs).

1. Compound (ii) forms a bridgehead carbocation in a bicyclo[3.3.1]nonane system. Because the rings are relatively large (two fused six-membered rings), the system is flexible enough to allow the bridgehead carbon to achieve a nearly planar $sp^2$ geometry. This makes the bicyclo[3.3.1]non-1-yl carbocation the most stable among the three, leading to the fastest rate of solvolysis.

2. Compound (iii) forms a 7-norbornyl (bicyclo[2.2.1]hept-7-yl) carbocation at the bridge carbon. This is a secondary carbocation that suffers from severe angle strain because the ideal $120^{\circ}$ bond angle for an $sp^2$ carbon is compressed to approximately $94^{\circ}$. However, it is slightly more stable than the 1-norbornyl carbocation.

3. Compound (i) forms a 1-norbornyl (bicyclo[2.2.1]hept-1-yl) carbocation. This is a bridgehead carbocation in a highly rigid and strained bicyclic system. According to Bredt's rule, forming a planar $sp^2$ center at this bridgehead introduces immense strain, making it extremely unstable and highly unreactive towards solvolysis.

Therefore, the stability of the carbocations and the corresponding rate of solvolysis follow the decreasing order: (ii) > (iii) > (i).

Answer: (ii) > (iii) > (i)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following reaction:



Identify the major product.
questions.solution:
The given reactant is 4,4-dibromo-3-methyl-1H-pyrazol-5(4H)-one. When treated with a strong base like aqueous $\mathrm{NaOH}$, it undergoes a ring-opening and fragmentation reaction to form an alkynoic acid.

The hydroxide ion ($\mathrm{OH^-}$) attacks the carbonyl carbon, leading to the cleavage of the amide-like $\mathrm{N-C}$ bond. This opens the ring to form a hydrazone intermediate with a carboxylate group: $\mathrm{NH_2-N=C(Me)-C(Br)_2-COO^-}$.

In the basic medium, the terminal nitrogen of the hydrazone is deprotonated to form an anion, $\mathrm{^-NH-N=C(Me)-C(Br)_2-COO^-}$.

This anion undergoes resonance to form a carbanion $\mathrm{HN=N-C^-(Me)-C(Br)_2-COO^-}$, which then expels a bromide ion ($\mathrm{Br^-}$) to form an azo-alkene intermediate: $\mathrm{HN=N-C(Me)=C(Br)-COO^-}$.

A second deprotonation by the base occurs at the nitrogen, yielding $\mathrm{^-N=N-C(Me)=C(Br)-COO^-}$.

This intermediate undergoes a concerted fragmentation. The lone pair on nitrogen forms a nitrogen-nitrogen triple bond (expelling $\mathrm{N_2}$ gas), the $\mathrm{N-C}$ bond breaks to form a carbon-carbon triple bond, and the second bromide ion is expelled as a leaving group. This yields the alkynoate ion: $\mathrm{Me-C \equiv C-COO^-}$.

Finally, acidification with $\mathrm{HCl}$ protonates the carboxylate to give 2-butynoic acid, $\mathrm{Me-C \equiv C-COOH}$.

Thus, the major product is $\mathrm{Me-C \equiv C-COOH}$.

Answer: $\mathrm{Me}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{COOH}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of reactivity of the following compounds towards an $\mathrm{E}2$ elimination reaction using alcoholic $\mathrm{KOH}$:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
For an $\text{E}2$ elimination reaction to occur in a cyclohexane ring, the leaving group and the $\beta$-hydrogen must be in an anti-periplanar arrangement, which means both must occupy axial positions.

Let's analyze the given structures:
The chlorine atom is located at $\text{C}_1$ and is pointing 'down'. For it to be axial, the ring must adopt a chair conformation where the 'down' position at $\text{C}_1$ is axial.
The adjacent carbons are $\text{C}_2$ and $\text{C}_6$.
At $\text{C}_6$, there are two methyl groups and no $\beta$-hydrogens, so elimination cannot occur towards $\text{C}_6$.
At $\text{C}_2$, there is a methyl group pointing 'down' and an isotope of hydrogen ($\text{D}$, $\text{H}$, or $\text{T}$) pointing 'up'.

In the chair conformation where $\text{Cl}$ is axial (down), the 'up' position at $\text{C}_2$ is also axial. Thus, the $\text{D/H/T}$ atom is axial and anti-periplanar to the axial $\text{Cl}$ atom. This conformation is also sterically favored because the alternative chair conformation would force the two methyl groups at $\text{C}_2$ and $\text{C}_6$ into a severe 1,3-diaxial interaction.

Since the only available $\beta$-hydrogen for elimination is the one at $\text{C}_2$ (which is $\text{D}$ in (i), $\text{H}$ in (ii), and $\text{T}$ in (iii)), the rate-determining step of the $\text{E}2$ reaction involves the cleavage of the $\text{C}-\text{H}$, $\text{C}-\text{D}$, or $\text{C}-\text{T}$ bond.

Due to the primary kinetic isotope effect, the bond strength increases with the mass of the isotope ($\text{C}-\text{H} < \text{C}-\text{D} < \text{C}-\text{T}$). Therefore, the $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bond breaks the fastest and the $\text{C}-\text{T}$ bond breaks the slowest.

The rate of $\text{E}2$ elimination will follow the order: $k_{\text{H}} > k_{\text{D}} > k_{\text{T}}$.
Thus, the correct order of reactivity is $\text{(ii)} > \text{(i)} > \text{(iii)}$.

Answer: $\text{(ii)} > \text{(i)} > \text{(iii)}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following statements are true for the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction of alkyl halides?
(I) Both the alkyl halide and the nucleophile are involved in the transition state of the rate-determining step.
(II) The reaction proceeds with inversion of configuration at the substitution centre.
(III) The reaction proceeds via the formation of a carbocation intermediate.
(IV) The order of reactivity of alkyl halides is $3^{\circ} > 2^{\circ} > 1^{\circ}$.
(V) The nucleophile must have an unshared electron pair and bear a negative charge.
(VI) Protic solvents favour the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction.
questions.solution:
Statement (I) is incorrect because the rate-determining step of an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reaction involves only the alkyl halide, not the nucleophile.

Statement (II) is incorrect because $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ reactions proceed with racemization (both retention and inversion of configuration), whereas $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reactions proceed with inversion.

Statement (III) is correct as the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ mechanism involves the heterolytic cleavage of the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}$ bond to form a carbocation intermediate.

Statement (IV) is correct because the reactivity in $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ depends on the stability of the carbocation intermediate, which follows the order $3^{\circ} > 2^{\circ} > 1^{\circ}$.

Statement (V) is incorrect because a nucleophile must have an unshared pair of electrons, but it can be neutral (e.g., $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$, $\mathrm{ROH}$) and does not necessarily need to bear a negative charge.

Statement (VI) is correct because polar protic solvents stabilize the carbocation intermediate and the leaving group through solvation, thereby favouring the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ mechanism.

Thus, statements (III), (IV), and (VI) are true.

Answer: (III), (IV) and (VI)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following two substitution reactions:

(I)

(II)

Which reaction proceeds at a faster rate, and what is the predominant mechanism for the formation of the given products?
questions.solution:
Both reactions involve a tertiary alkyl halide reacting with acetic acid, which is a weak nucleophile and a polar protic solvent. These conditions favor the $\mathrm{S_N1}$ mechanism, where the rate-determining step is the cleavage of the carbon-halogen bond to form a carbocation.

The rate of an $\mathrm{S_N1}$ reaction depends on the leaving group ability. The bromide ion ($\mathrm{Br^-}$) is a better leaving group than the chloride ion ($\mathrm{Cl^-}$) because the $\mathrm{C-Br}$ bond is weaker and more easily broken than the $\mathrm{C-Cl}$ bond.

Thus, tert-butyl bromide reacts faster than tert-butyl chloride. Reaction (I) proceeds at a faster rate, and the predominant mechanism is $\mathrm{S_N1}$.

Answer: Reaction (I), $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following changes will increase the rate of the elimination reaction in the given process?



(I) Using $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ instead of $50^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$
(II) Using water instead of ethanol
(III) Adding ethoxide to the ethanol
(IV) Using instead of
questions.solution:
The given reaction involves a tertiary alkyl chloride undergoing solvolysis in ethanol, which acts as both a solvent and a weak base/nucleophile. The reaction proceeds via $S_N1$ (yielding the ether) and $E1$ (yielding the alkene) mechanisms.

Let us analyze the effect of each change on the rate of the elimination reaction:

(I) Using $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ instead of $50^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$: Increasing the temperature increases the rate constant of the reaction. Furthermore, elimination reactions have a higher activation energy and a positive entropy of activation compared to substitution reactions, so higher temperatures disproportionately increase the rate of elimination.

(II) Using water instead of ethanol: Water is a more polar solvent than ethanol, which increases the rate of carbocation formation. However, water is a weaker base than ethanol, which tends to favor substitution ($S_N1$) over elimination ($E1$). Thus, it does not specifically increase the rate of elimination as effectively as the other changes.

(III) Adding ethoxide to the ethanol: Ethoxide ($\mathrm{EtO}^{-}$) is a strong base. Its addition shifts the elimination mechanism from unimolecular ($E1$) to bimolecular ($E2$). The rate of $E2$ elimination is directly proportional to the concentration of the strong base ($\text{Rate} = k[\text{RX}][\mathrm{EtO}^{-}]$), which significantly increases the overall rate of the elimination reaction.

(IV) Using the iodide derivative instead of the chloride derivative: Iodide ($\mathrm{I}^{-}$) is a much larger ion and a weaker base than chloride ($\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$), making it a better leaving group. A better leaving group lowers the activation energy for the cleavage of the carbon-halogen bond, thereby increasing the rate of both substitution and elimination reactions.

Therefore, changes (I), (III), and (IV) will increase the rate of the elimination reaction.

Answer: (I), (III) and (IV)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of reactivity of the following compounds towards nucleophilic aromatic substitution ($\mathrm{S_NAr}$):

I.
II.
III.
questions.solution:
Nucleophilic aromatic substitution ($\mathrm{S_NAr}$) is facilitated by the presence of electron-withdrawing groups (such as $-\mathrm{NO_2}$) at the ortho and para positions relative to the leaving group (halogen).

These groups stabilize the intermediate carbanion (Meisenheimer complex) through strong $-I$ and $-M$ effects. The greater the number of electron-withdrawing groups at the ortho and para positions, the more stable the intermediate, and consequently, the higher the reactivity of the compound towards $\mathrm{S_NAr}$.

Analyzing the given compounds:
Compound II (1-chloro-2,4,6-trinitrobenzene) has three $-\mathrm{NO_2}$ groups (two ortho, one para).
Compound I (1-chloro-2,4-dinitrobenzene) has two $-\mathrm{NO_2}$ groups (one ortho, one para).
Compound III (1-chloro-4-nitrobenzene) has one $-\mathrm{NO_2}$ group (para).

Since reactivity increases with the number of $-\mathrm{NO_2}$ groups at ortho and para positions, the correct order of reactivity is II > I > III.

Answer: II > I > III

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following reaction sequence:

Identify the correct statement regarding the products $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$.
options: $\mathrm{B}$ is optically inactive due to external compensation.
$\mathrm{B}$ does not have any chiral centres.
$\mathrm{B}$ is optically inactive due to internal compensation.
questions.solution:
The given starting material is meso-2,3-dibromobutane.

Reaction with $\mathrm{NaI}$ in acetone causes the dehalogenation of the vicinal dibromide. This reaction proceeds via an anti-elimination mechanism. Anti-elimination of the meso isomer yields the trans-alkene. Thus, product $\mathrm{A}$ is trans-2-butene.

Next, trans-2-butene reacts with trifluoroperacetic acid ($\mathrm{CF_3CO_3H}$). Peroxyacids undergo a concerted syn-addition to alkenes to form epoxides. Syn-addition of an oxygen atom to trans-2-butene yields trans-2,3-dimethyloxirane as product $\mathrm{B}$.

The trans-epoxide has two chiral centres and lacks any plane or centre of symmetry, making it a chiral molecule. Since the epoxidation can occur from either face of the planar alkene with equal probability, a racemic mixture of enantiomers is formed.

A racemic mixture is optically inactive due to external compensation.

Answer: $\mathrm{B}$ is optically inactive due to external compensation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following two reactions:

(i)


(ii)


Which of the two reactions proceeds at a faster rate, and what is the predominant mechanism for the formation of the alkene?
questions.solution:
The given reactions involve the elimination of $\mathrm{HCl}$ to form an alkene.

The reagent used is $80\% \text{ H}_2\text{O} / \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$, which is a polar protic solvent mixture containing weak bases/nucleophiles. The absence of a strong base and the use of a highly polar ionizing solvent strongly favor the $\mathrm{E}1$ mechanism over $\mathrm{E}2$.

In the $\mathrm{E}1$ mechanism, the rate-determining step is the heterolytic cleavage of the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond to form a carbocation intermediate.

For reaction (i), the reactant is 2-chloro-2-methylpentane. Loss of the chloride ion directly yields a tertiary ($3^{\circ}$) carbocation, which is highly stable.

For reaction (ii), the reactant is 3-chloro-2-methylpentane. Loss of the chloride ion initially yields a secondary ($2^{\circ}$) carbocation. Although this $2^{\circ}$ carbocation can undergo a hydride shift to form a more stable $3^{\circ}$ carbocation, the activation energy for the initial formation of the $2^{\circ}$ carbocation is higher than that for the direct formation of the $3^{\circ}$ carbocation in reaction (i).

Since the formation of the $3^{\circ}$ carbocation in reaction (i) is faster than the formation of the $2^{\circ}$ carbocation in reaction (ii), reaction (i) proceeds at a faster rate.

Therefore, reaction (i) is faster and the predominant mechanism is $\mathrm{E}1$.

Answer: Reaction (i), $\mathrm{E}1$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ solvolysis of the following halides in aqueous formic acid:

(i)

(ii)

(iii)

(iv)

Which of the following represents the correct decreasing order of their reactivity?
questions.solution:
The rate of an $\text{S}_{\text{N}}1$ solvolysis reaction depends on the stability of the intermediate carbocation formed after the departure of the leaving group (halide ion). Let us analyze the carbocations formed from each of the given substrates:

1. Halide (i) forms a secondary aliphatic carbocation, $(CH_3)_2CH-CH^+-CH_3$, which is stabilized by hyperconjugation ($4$ $\alpha$-hydrogens).
2. Halide (ii) forms a tertiary aliphatic carbocation, the $1$-methylcyclohexyl cation, which is stabilized by hyperconjugation ($7$ $\alpha$-hydrogens).
3. Halide (iii) forms a benzhydryl carbocation, $(C_6H_5)_2CH^+$, which is highly stabilized by extensive resonance delocalization over two phenyl rings.
4. Halide (iv) forms a secondary benzylic carbocation, the $1$-tetralyl cation, which is stabilized by resonance delocalization over one phenyl ring.

Comparing the stabilities of these carbocations:
- The benzhydryl cation (iii) is the most stable due to resonance with two benzene rings.
- The secondary benzylic cation (iv) is more stable than purely aliphatic carbocations due to resonance with one benzene ring.
- Among the aliphatic carbocations, the tertiary carbocation (ii) is more stable than the secondary carbocation (i) due to a greater number of $\alpha$-hydrogens contributing to hyperconjugation.

Therefore, the decreasing order of stability of the intermediate carbocations is (iii) > (iv) > (ii) > (i).

Since the reactivity in $\text{S}_{\text{N}}1$ reactions is directly proportional to the stability of the intermediate carbocation, the correct decreasing order of reactivity is (iii) > (iv) > (ii) > (i).

Answer: (iii) > (iv) > (ii) > (i)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following two substitution reactions:

Reaction I:


Reaction II:


Which of the following correctly identifies the faster reaction and its predominant mechanism?
options: Reaction II is faster and proceeds via an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ mechanism.
Reaction II is faster and proceeds via an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ mechanism.
Reaction I is faster and proceeds via an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ mechanism.
Reaction I is faster and proceeds via an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ mechanism.
questions.solution:
The given reactions involve 1-bromopropane, which is a primary alkyl halide. Primary alkyl halides undergo nucleophilic substitution predominantly via the $\mathrm{S_N2}$ mechanism due to minimal steric hindrance.

The solvent used is DMF (N,N-dimethylformamide), which is a polar aprotic solvent. Polar aprotic solvents favor $\mathrm{S_N2}$ reactions because they do not strongly solvate anions (nucleophiles) through hydrogen bonding, thereby increasing their reactivity.

To determine which reaction is faster, we must compare the nucleophilicity of the methoxide ion ($\mathrm{CH_3O^-}$) and the methanethiolate ion ($\mathrm{CH_3S^-}$).

Sulfur is larger and more polarizable than oxygen. In $\mathrm{S_N2}$ reactions, highly polarizable nucleophiles (soft nucleophiles) are better able to distort their electron clouds to initiate bond formation with the electrophilic $sp^3$ carbon (a soft electrophile) in the transition state.

Because of this higher polarizability, $\mathrm{CH_3S^-}$ is a significantly stronger nucleophile than $\mathrm{CH_3O^-}$ towards saturated carbon atoms, even in polar aprotic solvents.

Therefore, Reaction II is faster and proceeds via an $\mathrm{S_N2}$ mechanism.

Answer: Reaction II is faster and proceeds via an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ mechanism.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct reactivity order for the $\mathrm{E}2$ elimination reaction of the following compounds with alcoholic $\mathrm{KOH}$:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
The reactivity of alkyl halides towards E2 elimination with a strong base like alcoholic KOH depends primarily on the stability of the developing alkene character in the transition state. A more stable alkene product corresponds to a lower activation energy and a faster reaction rate.

Compound (i), 1-chloro-1-phenylpropane, is a secondary alkyl halide. Elimination of $\text{HCl}$ yields 1-phenylpropene. This is a disubstituted alkene that is highly stabilized by extended conjugation with the phenyl ring and hyperconjugation from the adjacent methyl group.

Compound (ii), 1-chloro-2-phenylethane, is a primary alkyl halide. The elimination involves the removal of a benzylic $\beta$-hydrogen to yield styrene. Styrene is a monosubstituted conjugated alkene. It is stabilized by conjugation but is less stable than the disubstituted conjugated alkene derived from compound (i).

Compound (iii), 1-chloropropane, is a simple primary alkyl halide. Elimination yields propene. This is an unconjugated, monosubstituted alkene and is the least stable of the three product alkenes.

Comparing the stability of the product alkenes, 1-phenylpropene is more stable than styrene, which in turn is more stable than propene. Therefore, the activation energy for the transition states follows the reverse order, making the reactivity order (i) > (ii) > (iii).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction is a solvolysis in aqueous acetone, which proceeds via an $S_{N}1$ mechanism.

The leaving group $I^{-}$ departs, generating a secondary carbocation on the cyclopentane ring.

This initial carbocation can undergo a 1,2-hydride shift from either of the adjacent carbon atoms.

A hydride shift from the carbon bearing the p-methoxyphenyl group generates a benzylic carbocation that is highly stabilized by the strong $+M$ (electron-donating) effect of the methoxy group.

A hydride shift from the carbon bearing the p-nitrophenyl group is highly unfavorable as it would generate a carbocation strongly destabilized by the $-M$ and $-I$ (electron-withdrawing) effects of the nitro group.

Therefore, the hydride shift occurs from the carbon with the p-methoxyphenyl group, moving the positive charge to that position.

The nucleophile ($H_{2}O$) then attacks this resonance-stabilized planar carbocation.

Due to steric hindrance from the bulky aryl groups located on the top face (wedge) of the ring, the attack of $H_{2}O$ preferentially occurs from the less hindered bottom face (dash).

This results in the $-OH$ group being oriented on a dash, while the p-methoxyphenyl group remains on a wedge.

This matches the structure shown in Option C.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
Step 1: The starting material is 2-(3-bromophenyl)-1,3-dioxolane. The 1,3-dioxolane ring is an acetal, which serves as a protecting group for the aldehyde functionality. Acetals are stable under basic conditions and will not react with Grignard reagents.

Step 2: In the first step, the reaction of the aryl bromide with magnesium (Mg) in ether forms a Grignard reagent. The bromine atom is converted to a -MgBr group, yielding 3-(1,3-dioxolan-2-yl)phenylmagnesium bromide.

Step 3: In the second step, ethanol (EtOH) is added. Grignard reagents are strong bases and will react with compounds containing acidic protons. Ethanol provides an acidic proton that quenches the Grignard reagent, replacing the -MgBr group with a hydrogen atom. This acid-base reaction yields 2-phenyl-1,3-dioxolane.

Step 4: In the third step, the addition of catalytic $H^+$ and $H_2O$ leads to the acidic hydrolysis of the acetal protecting group. The 1,3-dioxolane ring is cleaved, regenerating the original carbonyl group.

Step 5: The final major product formed after deprotection is benzaldehyde.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of reactivity of the following compounds towards nucleophilic aromatic substitution ($\mathrm{S_NAr}$):

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
Nucleophilic aromatic substitution ($\mathrm{S_NAr}$) proceeds via an addition-elimination mechanism, forming a carbanion intermediate (Meisenheimer complex). The reactivity depends on two main factors:
1. The presence of electron-withdrawing groups (EWGs) at ortho and para positions, which stabilize the intermediate carbanion and increase reactivity. Conversely, electron-donating groups (EDGs) destabilize the intermediate and decrease reactivity.
2. The nature of the leaving group. For $\mathrm{S_NAr}$, the reactivity order of halogens is $\mathrm{F} > \mathrm{Cl} > \mathrm{Br} > \mathrm{I}$ because the highly electronegative fluorine strongly polarizes the $\mathrm{C-X}$ bond, making the ipso carbon more electrophilic.

Analyzing the given compounds:
$\bullet$ Compound (I): Has two strong EWGs ($\mathrm{-NO_2}$) at the ortho and para positions, and fluorine as the leaving group. This provides maximum stabilization to the intermediate, making it the most reactive.
$\bullet$ Compound (III): Has one strong EWG ($\mathrm{-NO_2}$) at the para position and one EDG ($\mathrm{-CH_3}$) at the ortho position. It is moderately reactive, less than (I) but more than (II).
$\bullet$ Compound (II): Has two EDGs ($\mathrm{-CH_3}$) at the ortho and para positions. These groups destabilize the intermediate carbanion, making it the least reactive.

Therefore, the correct order of reactivity towards $\mathrm{S_NAr}$ is (I) > (III) > (II).

Answer: (I) > (III) > (II)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of reactivity of the following compounds towards nucleophilic aromatic substitution ($\mathrm{S_NAr}$):

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
In nucleophilic aromatic substitution ($\mathrm{S_NAr}$), the reaction proceeds via a two-step mechanism where the first step is the addition of the nucleophile to the halogen-bearing carbon to form a negatively charged intermediate (Meisenheimer complex). This step is the slow and rate-determining step.

The reactivity of the aryl halide depends on the stability of this intermediate carbanion. The more stabilized the negative charge, the faster the reaction.

Halogens stabilize the intermediate through their electron-withdrawing inductive effect ($-I$ effect). The electronegativity of halogens decreases down the group, so the strength of the $-I$ effect follows the order: $-\text{F} > -\text{Cl} > -\text{Br} > -\text{I}$.

Because fluorine is the most electronegative, it exerts the strongest $-I$ effect, making the carbon attached to it highly electrophilic and stabilizing the Meisenheimer complex to the greatest extent. Although the $\text{C}-\text{F}$ bond is the strongest, bond breaking is not involved in the rate-determining step.

Therefore, the order of reactivity towards $\mathrm{S_NAr}$ is $p$-fluoronitrobenzene > $p$-chloronitrobenzene > $p$-bromonitrobenzene.

This corresponds to the order (I) > (II) > (III).

Answer: (I) > (II) > (III)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following represents the correct order of reactivity towards an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction?
options: >
questions.solution:
The rate of an $S_N2$ reaction depends on the nature of the leaving group, the substrate structure, and the presence of substituents.

Comparing the leaving groups, bromide is a better leaving group than chloride. Thus, p-cyanobenzyl bromide is more reactive than p-cyanobenzyl chloride, and p-nitrobenzyl bromide is more reactive than p-nitrobenzyl chloride.

Aryl halides are highly unreactive towards $S_N2$ reactions due to the partial double bond character of the carbon-halogen bond and the $sp^2$ hybridized carbon atom. Benzyl halides are very reactive. Therefore, benzyl bromide is much more reactive than chlorobenzene.

For benzylic halides, the presence of electron-withdrawing groups at the para position increases the rate of $S_N2$ reactions by stabilizing the transition state. The $-CN$ group is a stronger electron-withdrawing group than the $-Cl$ group. Consequently, p-cyanobenzyl chloride is more reactive than p-chlorobenzyl chloride.

Answer: >

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction.
questions.solution:
The given reaction involves a secondary alkyl halide reacting with sodium hydrosulfide ($\text{NaSH}$).

$\text{NaSH}$ provides the $\text{SH}^{-}$ ion, which is a strong nucleophile. The reaction proceeds via an $\text{S}_{\text{N}}2$ mechanism.

Iodine ($\text{I}^{-}$) is an excellent leaving group, whereas $-\text{OH}$ and $-\text{OMe}$ are poor leaving groups. Therefore, nucleophilic substitution occurs exclusively at the carbon atom bonded to iodine.

An $\text{S}_{\text{N}}2$ reaction proceeds with complete inversion of configuration at the reacting center. Since the iodine atom is on a dashed bond, the incoming $-\text{SH}$ group will attach from the opposite face, resulting in a wedged bond.

The stereocenters bearing the $-\text{OH}$ and $-\text{OMe}$ groups do not participate in the reaction, so their configurations remain unchanged ($-\text{OH}$ remains wedged, $-\text{OMe}$ remains dashed).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of their reactivity towards nucleophilic aromatic substitution ($\mathrm{S_NAr}$):

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
The reactivity of aryl halides towards nucleophilic aromatic substitution ($S_NAr$) depends on the stability of the carbanion intermediate formed during the reaction.

Electron-withdrawing groups stabilize this intermediate and increase the rate of reaction.

A nitro group at the ortho or para position with respect to the leaving group stabilizes the intermediate strongly through both resonance ($-M$) and inductive ($-I$) effects.

A nitro group at the meta position stabilizes the intermediate only through the weaker inductive ($-I$) effect, as the negative charge in the intermediate does not delocalize onto the meta position.

Compound (II) contains two nitro groups located at the ortho and para positions, providing the maximum stabilization to the intermediate. Thus, it is the most reactive.

Compound (III) contains one nitro group at the para position, providing both $-M$ and $-I$ stabilization. It is less reactive than (II) but more reactive than (I).

Compound (I) contains one nitro group at the meta position, providing only $-I$ stabilization. Therefore, it is the least reactive.

The decreasing order of reactivity is (II) > (III) > (I).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A compound A with the molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{3}\mathrm{H}_{6}\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ on reaction with alkali can give either compound B ($\mathrm{C}_{3}\mathrm{H}_{6}\mathrm{O}$) or compound C ($\mathrm{C}_{3}\mathrm{H}_{4}$) depending on the reaction conditions. Compound B on oxidation gives a compound of formula $\mathrm{C}_{3}\mathrm{H}_{6}\mathrm{O}_{2}$. Compound C upon treatment with dilute $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$ containing $\mathrm{Hg}^{2+}$ ions gives compound D ($\mathrm{C}_{3}\mathrm{H}_{6}\mathrm{O}$). Compound D reacts with bromine and alkali to give the sodium salt of an acid having the formula $\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{4}\mathrm{O}_{2}$. Identify compound A.
questions.solution:
Compound D reacts with bromine and alkali to give the sodium salt of an acid with formula $\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{4}\mathrm{O}_{2}$ ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOH}$). This indicates that D is a methyl ketone and undergoes the haloform reaction. Since D has the formula $\mathrm{C}_{3}\mathrm{H}_{6}\mathrm{O}$, D must be acetone ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCH}_{3}$).

Compound D is formed by the hydration of compound C ($\mathrm{C}_{3}\mathrm{H}_{4}$) in the presence of dilute $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{Hg}^{2+}$ ions. This means C is an alkyne. For C to yield acetone upon hydration, C must be propyne ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{CH}$).

Compound A ($\mathrm{C}_{3}\mathrm{H}_{6}\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$) gives propyne (C) upon dehydrohalogenation with an alkali. Thus, A can be a geminal or vicinal dihalide.

Compound A also reacts with aqueous alkali to give compound B ($\mathrm{C}_{3}\mathrm{H}_{6}\mathrm{O}$), which upon oxidation yields an acid with formula $\mathrm{C}_{3}\mathrm{H}_{6}\mathrm{O}_{2}$ (propanoic acid, $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{COOH}$). This implies that B is an aldehyde, specifically propanal ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CHO}$).

For compound A to yield propanal upon hydrolysis, both halogen atoms must be attached to the terminal carbon atom. Therefore, A is 1,1-dichloropropane ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CHCl}_{2}$).

The reactions are as follows:

$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CHCl}_{2} \xrightarrow{\text{aq. KOH}} \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CHO}$ (B)

$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CHO} \xrightarrow{[\mathrm{O}]} \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{COOH}$ ($\mathrm{C}_{3}\mathrm{H}_{6}\mathrm{O}_{2}$)

$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CHCl}_{2} \xrightarrow{\text{alc. KOH}} \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{CH}$ (C)

$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{CH} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Hg}^{2+}, \mathrm{H}^{+}} \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCH}_{3}$ (D)

$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCH}_{3} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Br}_{2}, \mathrm{NaOH}} \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COONa} + \mathrm{CHBr}_{3}$

Answer: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CHCl}_{2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of reactivity of the following compounds towards nucleophilic aromatic substitution ($\mathrm{S_NAr}$):

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
Nucleophilic aromatic substitution ($\mathrm{S_NAr}$) proceeds via an addition-elimination mechanism. The reactivity is highly enhanced by the presence of strong electron-withdrawing groups (EWGs) at the ortho and para positions relative to the leaving group, as they stabilize the intermediate carbanion (Meisenheimer complex) through both $-M$ and $-I$ effects.

Furthermore, the rate of $\mathrm{S_NAr}$ depends on the electronegativity of the halogen leaving group. A more electronegative halogen makes the attached carbon more electrophilic and stabilizes the transition state during the addition step. The general order of leaving group ability in $\mathrm{S_NAr}$ is $\mathrm{F} > \mathrm{Cl} > \mathrm{Br} > \mathrm{I}$.

Analyzing the given compounds:
Compound (i): 1-Fluoro-2,4-dinitrobenzene has two strongly electron-withdrawing $-\mathrm{NO_2}$ groups at the ortho and para positions. These groups provide strong $-M$ and $-I$ effects, highly stabilizing the intermediate. Additionally, fluorine is the most effective leaving group for the addition step of $\mathrm{S_NAr}$. Thus, it is the most reactive.

Compound (iii): 1-Bromo-3,5-dinitrobenzene has two $-\mathrm{NO_2}$ groups at the meta positions. At the meta position, $-\mathrm{NO_2}$ groups can only stabilize the intermediate via the $-I$ effect (no $-M$ effect). Therefore, it is less reactive than compound (i).

Compound (ii): 1-Chloro-2,4-dimethylbenzene has two $-\mathrm{CH_3}$ groups at the ortho and para positions. Methyl groups are electron-donating ($+I$ and hyperconjugation), which destabilize the intermediate carbanion and decrease the electrophilicity of the ring. Thus, it is the least reactive.

Therefore, the correct order of reactivity towards $\mathrm{S_NAr}$ is (i) > (iii) > (ii).

Answer: (i) > (iii) > (ii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When cyclohexyl chloride reacts with ammonia ($\mathrm{NH}_{3}$) to form cyclohexylamine, the reaction does not go to completion if only one equivalent of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is used. Which of the following best explains this observation?
options: Chloride is a poor leaving group, so more $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is needed to increase the reaction rate.
The cyclohexyl group presents significant steric hindrance, requiring an excess of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ to proceed.
The $\mathrm{HCl}$ generated during the reaction protonates $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$, forming $\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}$ which is not nucleophilic.
$\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is a weak nucleophile, so a large excess is needed to drive the equilibrium forward.
questions.solution:
The reaction of cyclohexyl chloride with ammonia proceeds via nucleophilic substitution:

$\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{11}\mathrm{Cl} + \mathrm{NH}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{11}\mathrm{NH}_{2} + \mathrm{HCl}$

The $\mathrm{HCl}$ produced is a strong acid and immediately reacts with the unreacted basic ammonia ($\mathrm{NH}_{3}$) to form ammonium chloride:

$\mathrm{NH}_{3} + \mathrm{HCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+} + \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$

The ammonium ion ($\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}$) lacks a lone pair of electrons and is therefore not nucleophilic. Because one molecule of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is consumed by the $\mathrm{HCl}$ produced for every molecule of cyclohexyl chloride that reacts, using only one equivalent of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ will cause the reaction to stop at approximately $50\%$ conversion. To drive the reaction to completion, at least two equivalents of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ (or an additional base) are required to neutralize the generated $\mathrm{HCl}$.

Answer: The $\mathrm{HCl}$ generated during the reaction protonates $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$, forming $\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}$ which is not nucleophilic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product $\mathrm{P}$ in the following reaction:

$\xrightarrow[\text{(ii) } \mathrm{H}^{\oplus}]{\text{(i) } \mathrm{HO}^{\ominus} / \Delta}$ $\mathrm{P}$
questions.solution:
The given reaction is a nucleophilic aromatic substitution ($S_NAr$).

The $-NO_2$ group is strongly electron-withdrawing and activates the benzene ring towards nucleophilic attack at the ortho and para positions. This is because the intermediate carbanion (Meisenheimer complex) is stabilized by resonance when the attack occurs at these positions.

In the starting material (1,2-dichloro-4-nitrobenzene), the chlorine atom at position 1 is para to the $-NO_2$ group, whereas the chlorine atom at position 2 is meta to the $-NO_2$ group.

Attack of the hydroxide ion ($HO^{\ominus}$) at the para position (C1) allows the negative charge of the intermediate to be delocalized onto the $-NO_2$ group, providing significant stabilization. Attack at the meta position (C2) does not allow this delocalization.

Thus, the chlorine atom para to the $-NO_2$ group is selectively substituted by the $-OH$ group.

The major product is 2-chloro-4-nitrophenol, matching the structure in option (A).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Among the following compounds, which one is most readily hydrolyzed by aqueous alkali?
questions.solution:
The hydrolysis of aryl chlorides by aqueous alkali proceeds via the nucleophilic aromatic substitution ($S_NAr$) mechanism. The rate-determining step is the attack of the nucleophile ($OH^-$) on the carbon bearing the halogen, forming a negatively charged intermediate (Meisenheimer complex).

The presence of electron-withdrawing groups (like $-NO_2$) on the benzene ring stabilizes this intermediate carbanion. This stabilization is most effective when the electron-withdrawing groups are at the ortho and/or para positions relative to the halogen, as they can stabilize the negative charge through both $-I$ (inductive) and $-M$ (mesomeric/resonance) effects.

Let us analyze the given options:
(A) 1-chloro-2,4-dinitrobenzene has two $-NO_2$ groups at the ortho and para positions. Both exert strong $-I$ and $-M$ effects, highly stabilizing the intermediate.
(B) 1-chloro-3,5-dinitrobenzene has two $-NO_2$ groups at the meta positions. They stabilize the intermediate only through the $-I$ effect, as the $-M$ effect does not operate from the meta position.
(C) 1-chloro-2-nitrobenzene has one $-NO_2$ group at the ortho position, providing $-I$ and $-M$ stabilization, but less than two groups.
(D) 1-chloro-3-nitrobenzene has one $-NO_2$ group at the meta position, providing only $-I$ stabilization.

Since compound (A) has the maximum number of electron-withdrawing $-NO_2$ groups at the most effective positions (ortho and para), its intermediate is the most stable. Therefore, it undergoes nucleophilic substitution most readily.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction.
questions.solution:
The given reaction is a nucleophilic aromatic substitution ($S_{N}Ar$) where the methoxide ion ($CH_{3}O^{-}$) acts as the nucleophile.

The substrate, 1,2-dichloro-3,5-dinitrobenzene, has two chlorine atoms that can potentially act as leaving groups. The regioselectivity of the reaction is determined by the stability of the intermediate Meisenheimer complex (carbanion).

Numbering the carbon atoms of the benzene ring starting with the top chlorine as C1 and the left chlorine as C2, the nitro groups are at C3 and C5.

If the nucleophile attacks at C1, the negative charge in the intermediate is delocalized to the ortho (C2, C6) and para (C4) positions. The nitro groups at C3 and C5 are meta to the site of attack and can only stabilize the intermediate via the inductive effect ($-I$).

If the nucleophile attacks at C2, the negative charge is delocalized to the ortho (C1, C3) and para (C5) positions. Here, the nitro groups are located exactly at C3 and C5. They strongly stabilize the intermediate carbanion through both inductive ($-I$) and resonance ($-M$) effects.

Because the intermediate formed by attack at C2 is significantly more stable, the substitution occurs preferentially at this position. The chlorine atom at C2 is replaced by the methoxy group ($-OMe$).

Therefore, the major product is 1-chloro-2-methoxy-3,5-dinitrobenzene.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds is most reactive towards nucleophilic aromatic substitution with $\mathrm{NaOMe}$?
questions.solution:
The given reaction is a nucleophilic aromatic substitution ($\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}\mathrm{Ar}$).

The reactivity of aryl halides towards $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}\mathrm{Ar}$ increases with the presence of strong electron-withdrawing groups (such as $-\mathrm{NO}_2$) at the ortho and para positions with respect to the halogen atom. These groups stabilize the intermediate carbanion (Meisenheimer complex) via the $-R$ effect.

In 1-fluoro-3,4-dinitrobenzene and 1-chloro-3,4-dinitrobenzene, one $-\mathrm{NO}_2$ group is at the meta position and the other is at the para position. The meta $-\mathrm{NO}_2$ group cannot stabilize the intermediate through resonance.

In 1-bromo-2,4-dinitrobenzene and 1-fluoro-2,4-dinitrobenzene, the $-\mathrm{NO}_2$ groups are at the ortho and para positions, which strongly activate the ring towards nucleophilic attack.

Comparing 1-bromo-2,4-dinitrobenzene and 1-fluoro-2,4-dinitrobenzene, the rate-determining step in $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}\mathrm{Ar}$ is the addition of the nucleophile to the aromatic ring. The highly electronegative fluorine atom exerts a strong $-I$ effect, making the ipso carbon more electrophilic and stabilizing the anionic transition state much better than bromine. Thus, the reactivity order for the leaving group in $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}\mathrm{Ar}$ is $\mathrm{F} > \mathrm{Cl} > \mathrm{Br} > \mathrm{I}$.

Therefore, 1-fluoro-2,4-dinitrobenzene is the most reactive compound.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following nucleophilic aromatic substitution reactions:
(I)
(II)
(III)
Choose the correct order of reactivity for these reactions.
questions.solution:
The given reactions proceed via the nucleophilic aromatic substitution ($S_NAr$) mechanism.

In an $S_NAr$ reaction, the rate-determining step is the addition of the nucleophile to the halogen-bearing carbon to form a carbanion intermediate (Meisenheimer complex). The carbon-halogen bond breaking is not involved in this slow step.

The reactivity is governed by the electron-withdrawing inductive effect ($-I$ effect) of the halogen, which increases the electrophilicity of the carbon atom and stabilizes the intermediate carbanion.

The $-I$ effect of halogens decreases in the order: $F > Cl > Br > I$.

Therefore, the fluoro-substituted compound forms the most stable intermediate and reacts the fastest. The correct order of reactivity is (I) > (II) > (III).

Answer: (I) > (II) > (III)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction that is favoured (i.e., its rate increases) in a polar protic medium.
questions.solution:
The effect of a solvent on the rate of an $S_N2$ reaction depends on how it stabilizes the reactants compared to the transition state (Hughes-Ingold rules).

In option (A), both reactants ($\text{NH}_3$ and $\text{Me-I}$) are neutral. During the formation of the transition state, a partial positive charge develops on the nitrogen atom and a partial negative charge develops on the leaving iodine atom ($[\text{H}_3\text{N}^{\delta+} \cdots \text{Me} \cdots \text{I}^{\delta-}]^\ddagger$). A polar protic solvent stabilizes this charge-separated transition state much more than the neutral reactants through solvation and hydrogen bonding. This lowers the activation energy, thereby increasing the rate of the reaction.

In option (B), the reactant $\text{Me}_3\text{S}^+$ is charged, and the positive charge gets dispersed in the transition state. A polar solvent stabilizes the localized charge on the reactant more than the dispersed charge in the transition state, which decreases the rate.

In option (C), the nucleophile $\text{OH}^-$ is charged. A polar protic solvent strongly solvates the $\text{OH}^-$ ion via hydrogen bonding, lowering its ground state energy and reducing its nucleophilicity. This increases the activation energy and decreases the rate.

In option (D), the reactants are oppositely charged ($\text{OH}^-$ and $\text{Me}_4\text{N}^+$) and the charges neutralize in the transition state. A polar solvent strongly stabilizes the charged reactants, significantly decreasing the reaction rate.

Therefore, only the reaction in option (A) is favored and its rate increases in a polar protic medium.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The given reaction sequence involves three main steps:

Step 1: The starting material, 2-(3-bromophenyl)-1,3-dioxolane, reacts with magnesium (Mg) in the presence of ether. This forms a Grignard reagent at the position of the bromine atom, yielding 3-(1,3-dioxolan-2-yl)phenylmagnesium bromide. The acetal group is stable under these basic conditions and protects the aldehyde functionality.

Step 2: Ethanol (EtOH) is added to the reaction mixture. Grignard reagents are strong bases and react readily with compounds containing acidic protons, such as alcohols. The Grignard reagent abstracts a proton from ethanol, undergoing an acid-base reaction to form 2-phenyl-1,3-dioxolane and an ethoxymagnesium bromide salt. Effectively, the -MgBr group is replaced by a hydrogen atom.

Step 3: The addition of catalytic $H^+$ and $H_2O$ results in the acidic hydrolysis of the acetal group. The 1,3-dioxolane ring is cleaved, regenerating the carbonyl group. Thus, 2-phenyl-1,3-dioxolane is converted into benzaldehyde.

The final major product is benzaldehyde.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product of the following reaction:

$\xrightarrow[\text{(ii) } \mathrm{H}_3\mathrm{O}^+]{\text{(i) } \mathrm{NaH}}$ ?
questions.solution:
The given reactant is 1-allyl-4-iodo-3-(iodomethyl)indolin-6-ol. The reaction proceeds through the following steps:

1. Sodium hydride ($\mathrm{NaH}$) acts as a strong base and deprotonates the acidic phenolic $-\mathrm{OH}$ group to form a phenoxide ion.

2. The electron-rich phenoxide ion undergoes an intramolecular nucleophilic attack (Winstein spiroalkylation) on the adjacent $-\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{I}$ group. This displaces the iodide ion ($\mathrm{I}^-$) and forms a highly reactive spiro-cyclopropane intermediate fused to a cyclohexadienone system.

3. The spiro-cyclopropane intermediate is extremely electrophilic due to the strong driving force for rearomatization. Excess $\mathrm{NaH}$ acts as a nucleophilic hydride ($\mathrm{H}^-$) donor, which attacks the cyclopropane ring. This opens the ring, rearomatizes the system back to a phenoxide, and effectively reduces the $-\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{I}$ group to a methyl group ($-\mathrm{CH}_3$).

4. The aryl iodide at the C4 position is unactivated and remains completely unaffected under these conditions.

5. Finally, the acidic workup ($\mathrm{H}_3\mathrm{O}^+$) protonates the phenoxide back to the phenol.

The major product is 1-allyl-4-iodo-3-methylindolin-6-ol, where the alkyl iodide is selectively reduced to a methyl group while the aryl iodide remains intact.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An aromatic compound $\mathbf{A}$ ($\mathrm{C}_{7}\mathrm{H}_{6}\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$) gives a precipitate of $\mathrm{AgCl}$ on boiling with an alcoholic $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ solution and yields a compound with the formula $\mathrm{C}_{7}\mathrm{H}_{7}\mathrm{OCl}$ on treatment with aqueous $\mathrm{NaOH}$. Compound $\mathbf{A}$ on oxidation gives a monochlorobenzoic acid which affords only one mononitro derivative. Compound $\mathbf{A}$ is:
questions.solution:
Compound $\mathbf{A}$ ($\mathrm{C}_{7}\mathrm{H}_{6}\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$) gives a precipitate of $\mathrm{AgCl}$ on boiling with alcoholic $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ solution, which indicates the presence of a reactive benzylic chlorine atom.

Treatment of $\mathbf{A}$ with aqueous $\mathrm{NaOH}$ yields a compound with the formula $\mathrm{C}_{7}\mathrm{H}_{7}\mathrm{OCl}$. This implies that only one chlorine atom is hydrolyzed to an $-\mathrm{OH}$ group, while the other chlorine atom is directly attached to the benzene ring (aryl chloride) and remains unreactive. Thus, compound $\mathbf{A}$ is a chlorobenzyl chloride, $\mathrm{Cl}-\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{4}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{Cl}$.

Oxidation of $\mathbf{A}$ gives a monochlorobenzoic acid, $\mathrm{Cl}-\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{4}-\mathrm{COOH}$.

This monochlorobenzoic acid affords only one mononitro derivative upon nitration. We can determine the correct isomer by analyzing the directing effects in the nitration of chlorobenzoic acids:
$\bullet$ In $o$-chlorobenzoic acid, the $-\mathrm{COOH}$ group (meta-directing) and $-\mathrm{Cl}$ group (ortho/para-directing) direct the electrophile to positions 3 and 5. Since these positions are non-equivalent, it yields two mononitro derivatives.
$\bullet$ In $m$-chlorobenzoic acid, the directing groups lead to substitution at multiple non-equivalent positions, yielding a mixture of several mononitro derivatives.
$\bullet$ In $p$-chlorobenzoic acid, the $-\mathrm{COOH}$ group directs to positions 3 and 5, and the $-\mathrm{Cl}$ group also directs to positions 3 and 5. Because the molecule has an axis of symmetry passing through C1 and C4, positions 3 and 5 are identical. Therefore, nitration yields only one mononitro derivative (4-chloro-3-nitrobenzoic acid).

Since the oxidation product is $p$-chlorobenzoic acid, the original compound $\mathbf{A}$ must be $p$-chlorobenzyl chloride.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product of the following reaction:

$\xrightarrow[\text{Dioxane}]{\mathrm{NaOD} / \mathrm{D}_2\mathrm{O}}$ ?
questions.solution:
The given reaction is an example of the Ramberg-Backlund rearrangement.

Due to the presence of the electron-withdrawing sulfone group, the $\alpha$-hydrogen atoms are acidic. When treated with $\mathrm{NaOD}$ in $\mathrm{D}_2\mathrm{O}$, rapid hydrogen-deuterium exchange takes place before the rearrangement. The $-\mathrm{CH}_2-$ and $-\mathrm{CHCl}-$ groups are completely deuterated to form $-\mathrm{CD}_2-$ and $-\mathrm{CDCl}-$.

The base $\mathrm{OD}^-$ then abstracts a deuterium cation ($\mathrm{D}^+$) from the $-\mathrm{CD}_2-$ group, generating a carbanion.

This carbanion undergoes an intramolecular nucleophilic substitution, displacing the chloride ion to form a three-membered episulfone intermediate. The two carbons of the episulfone ring each bear one deuterium atom.

Finally, the episulfone undergoes cheletropic extrusion of sulfur dioxide ($\mathrm{SO}_2$) to yield a carbon-carbon double bond. The resulting product is 9,10-dideuterophenanthrene.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction is an example of an intramolecular Friedel-Crafts alkylation.

Step 1: The Lewis acid $\text{AlCl}_3$ reacts with the terminal alkyl chloride to generate a primary carbocation (or a highly polarized electrophilic complex).
$\text{Ph-C(=O)-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-Cl} + \text{AlCl}_3 \rightarrow \text{Ph-C(=O)-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2^+ + \text{AlCl}_4^-$

Step 2: The generated electrophilic center undergoes an intramolecular attack on the adjacent benzene ring. Although the carbonyl group is deactivating and meta-directing, intramolecular reactions that form stable 5- or 6-membered rings are highly favored due to entropy. The electrophile attacks the ortho position of the benzene ring.

Step 3: The attack forms a new 6-membered ring fused to the benzene ring. The intermediate sigma complex loses a proton to restore aromaticity, yielding $\alpha$-tetralone (3,4-dihydronaphthalen-1(2H)-one).
