Step 4: The addition of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ in the second step is to quench the reaction mixture and hydrolyze the complex formed between the Lewis acid and the carbonyl oxygen of the product.

The final major product is $\alpha$-tetralone, which corresponds to option (D).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of reactivity of the following compounds towards nucleophilic aromatic substitution ($\mathrm{S_NAr}$):

(i)

(ii)

(iii)
questions.solution:
In nucleophilic aromatic substitution ($\mathrm{S_NAr}$), the reaction proceeds via a two-step mechanism where the addition of the nucleophile to the aromatic ring to form a carbanion intermediate (Meisenheimer complex) is the rate-determining step.

The rate of the reaction depends on the electrophilicity of the carbon atom attached to the leaving group and the stabilization of the intermediate carbanion. Since the carbon-halogen bond breaking is not involved in the rate-determining step, the leaving group ability (bond strength) does not dictate the reactivity order.

Halogens exert an electron-withdrawing inductive effect ($-I$ effect), which increases the electrophilicity of the ipso carbon and stabilizes the intermediate carbanion. The $-I$ effect follows the order of electronegativity: $\text{F} > \text{Cl} > \text{Br} > \text{I}$.

Because fluorine is the most electronegative, 1-fluoro-2,4-dinitrobenzene has the most electrophilic carbon and forms the most stable intermediate. Thus, the reactivity order towards $\mathrm{S_NAr}$ is $\text{Ar-F} > \text{Ar-Cl} > \text{Ar-Br}$.

Therefore, the correct order of reactivity is (i) > (ii) > (iii).

Answer: (i) > (ii) > (iii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of their reactivity towards nucleophilic aromatic substitution:

I.      II.

III.      IV.
questions.solution:
The reactivity of aryl and heteroaryl halides towards nucleophilic aromatic substitution (S$_N$Ar) depends on the stability of the intermediate carbanion (Meisenheimer complex). Electron-withdrawing groups (EWGs) at the ortho and para positions strongly stabilize this intermediate through resonance (-M effect), thereby increasing the rate of reaction.

Let us analyze the given compounds:

1. Compound IV (2-chloro-5-nitropyridine): The leaving group (Cl) is at the 2-position. The intermediate carbanion is stabilized by both the electronegative ring nitrogen (which is ortho to the Cl) and the strongly electron-withdrawing nitro group (which is para to the Cl). Both groups exert strong -M and -I effects, making this intermediate the most stable. Thus, IV is the most reactive.

2. Compound II (4-chloronitrobenzene): The nitro group is para to the chlorine atom. It strongly stabilizes the negative charge of the intermediate through resonance (-M effect) and inductive effect (-I effect).

3. Compound I (2-chloropyridine): The ring nitrogen is ortho to the chlorine atom and stabilizes the intermediate by accommodating the negative charge. However, a nitro group is a stronger electron-withdrawing group than a pyridine nitrogen. Therefore, the intermediate of 4-chloronitrobenzene is more stable than that of 2-chloropyridine, making II more reactive than I.

4. Compound III (3-chloronitrobenzene): The nitro group is meta to the chlorine atom. At the meta position, the nitro group cannot stabilize the negative charge through resonance (-M effect); it only exerts a weaker -I effect. Thus, the intermediate is the least stable, making III the least reactive.

Comparing the stabilities of the intermediates, the decreasing order of reactivity towards S$_N$Ar is:
IV > II > I > III

Answer: IV > II > I > III

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction.
options: I
questions.solution:
The given reaction is a Finkelstein reaction, which involves the substitution of a chloride or bromide with an iodide using NaI in acetone. This reaction proceeds via an $S_N2$ mechanism.

$S_N2$ reactions are highly sensitive to steric hindrance and occur preferentially at the less hindered carbon atom.

In the given substrate, there are two possible leaving groups: the chlorine atom at C1 and the chlorine atom at C4.

The carbon C1 is flanked by two methyl groups at the adjacent C2 and C6 positions. These bulky methyl groups create significant steric hindrance, blocking the approach of the nucleophile ($I^-$) from the backside.

On the other hand, the carbon C4 is flanked by two less bulky $CH_2$ groups at the C3 and C5 positions, making it significantly less sterically hindered compared to C1.

Therefore, the nucleophilic attack by $I^-$ will selectively occur at the less hindered C4 position.

Since the reaction follows an $S_N2$ pathway, it will proceed with complete inversion of configuration at the reacting center (C4).

In the reactant, the chlorine atom at C4 is on a wedged bond (pointing upwards). After the $S_N2$ attack, the incoming iodine atom will be on a dashed bond (pointing downwards) due to inversion.

The stereocenters at C1, C2, and C6 do not participate in the reaction, so their stereochemistry remains unchanged (the top Cl remains dashed, and the two methyl groups remain wedged).

This leads to the product shown in option (D).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of their reactivity towards nucleophilic aromatic substitution:

(I)
(II)
(III)
(IV)
questions.solution:
Nucleophilic aromatic substitution ($S_NAr$) is facilitated by the presence of electron-withdrawing groups (EWGs) at the ortho and para positions relative to the leaving group (halogen).

The $-NO_2$ group is a strong electron-withdrawing group due to its $-M$ and $-I$ effects. It stabilizes the intermediate carbanion (Meisenheimer complex) formed during the rate-determining step of the reaction.

The greater the number of $-NO_2$ groups at the ortho and para positions, the more stable the intermediate carbanion, and consequently, the higher the reactivity of the compound towards nucleophilic aromatic substitution.

Analyzing the given compounds:
(I) Chlorobenzene has no $-NO_2$ groups.
(II) 1-Chloro-2-nitrobenzene has one $-NO_2$ group at the ortho position.
(III) 1-Chloro-2,4-dinitrobenzene has two $-NO_2$ groups at the ortho and para positions.
(IV) 1-Chloro-2,4,6-trinitrobenzene has three $-NO_2$ groups at the ortho and para positions.

Therefore, the decreasing order of reactivity is (IV) > (III) > (II) > (I).

Answer: (IV) > (III) > (II) > (I)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following phosphorus ylides is not a practical reagent for the Wittig reaction because its precursor phosphonium salt is difficult to prepare via a standard $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction?
questions.solution:
The preparation of a phosphorus ylide for the Wittig reaction involves the nucleophilic substitution ($\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$) of an alkyl halide by triphenylphosphine ($\mathrm{PPh}_{3}$), followed by deprotonation.

For the ylide $(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{3}\mathrm{CCH}=\mathrm{PPh}_{3}$, the required precursor alkyl halide is a neopentyl halide, $(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{3}\mathrm{CCH}_{2}\mathrm{X}$. Neopentyl halides are highly sterically hindered due to the bulky tert-butyl group adjacent to the $\alpha$-carbon. This severe steric hindrance makes the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ attack by the bulky triphenylphosphine extremely slow and practically unfeasible.

For the ylide $\mathrm{Ph}_{3}\stackrel{+}{\mathrm{P}}\stackrel{-}{\mathrm{C}}\mathrm{HCH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{3}$, the precursor is a primary alkyl halide ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{X}$), which readily undergoes $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reactions.

For the ylide $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{COCH}=\mathrm{PPh}_{3}$, the precursor is an $\alpha$-halo ketone ($\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{COCH}_{2}\mathrm{X}$), which is highly activated towards $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reactions.

For the ylide $\mathrm{Ph}_{3}\mathrm{P}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$, the precursor is a methyl halide ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{X}$), which is the most reactive substrate for $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reactions.

Thus, $(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{3}\mathrm{CCH}=\mathrm{PPh}_{3}$ is not a practical reagent because its precursor phosphonium salt is difficult to prepare.

Answer: $(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{3}\mathrm{CCH}=\mathrm{PPh}_{3}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the reagent required for the second step of the given reaction sequence.
questions.solution:
The first step involves the reaction of chlorobenzene with $\text{NaNH}_2$ in liquid $\text{NH}_3$, which generates benzyne as a reactive intermediate via an elimination mechanism.

In the second step, benzyne acts as a dienophile and undergoes a Diels-Alder reaction with a diene to form the given bicyclic product.

The product, 1,4-epoxy-1,4-dihydronaphthalene, contains an oxygen bridge. This indicates that the diene must be a cyclic ether with two conjugated double bonds.

Furan is a five-membered heterocyclic diene containing an oxygen atom. The Diels-Alder reaction between benzyne and furan yields the exact product shown.

Therefore, the required reagent is furan.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the two main products formed in the following reaction.
questions.solution:
The reaction of $p$-bromotoluene with $\text{NaNH}_2$ in liquid $\text{NH}_3$ proceeds via the benzyne mechanism.

The strong base $\text{NH}_2^-$ abstracts a proton from the position ortho to the bromine atom, followed by the elimination of the bromide ion to form a benzyne intermediate (4-methylbenzyne).

The nucleophile $\text{NH}_2^-$ can then attack either of the two carbon atoms of the triple bond (C3 or C4) in the benzyne intermediate.

Attack at the C4 position gives $p$-toluidine.

Attack at the C3 position gives $m$-toluidine.

Therefore, the two main products are $p$-toluidine and $m$-toluidine.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The reaction of 3-bromotoluene with magnesium in dry ether ($\text{Et}_2\text{O}$) forms the Grignard reagent, 3-methylphenylmagnesium bromide.

This Grignard reagent acts as a strong base. Upon treatment with heavy water ($\text{D}_2\text{O}$), the nucleophilic carbon attached to magnesium abstracts a deuterium ion ($\text{D}^+$) from $\text{D}_2\text{O}$.

This acid-base reaction results in the replacement of the $-\text{MgBr}$ group with a deuterium atom, yielding 3-deuterotoluene as the final product.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product of the following reaction sequence.



(i) $\mathrm{Mg}, \mathrm{Et}_{2}\mathrm{O}$
(ii) $\mathrm{CD}_{2}\mathrm{O}$
(iii) $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$
questions.solution:
The given reaction sequence involves the formation of a Grignard reagent followed by its nucleophilic addition to a carbonyl compound and subsequent hydrolysis.

In the first step, the reaction of 2-bromotoluene with magnesium ($\mathrm{Mg}$) in dry ether ($\mathrm{Et}_{2}\mathrm{O}$) forms the Grignard reagent, 2-methylphenylmagnesium bromide.

In the second step, the Grignard reagent acts as a nucleophile and attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of deuterated formaldehyde ($\mathrm{CD}_{2}\mathrm{O}$). This nucleophilic addition results in the formation of an alkoxide intermediate, where the aromatic ring is attached to a $-\mathrm{CD}_{2}-\mathrm{O}^{-}\mathrm{MgBr}^{+}$ group.

In the third step, hydrolysis of this intermediate with water ($\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$) protonates the alkoxide oxygen. The final product is 2-(methyl)phenyl-dideuteromethanol, which has a $-\mathrm{CD}_{2}\mathrm{OH}$ group ortho to the methyl group on the benzene ring.

Therefore, the major product is represented by the structure in option (C).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The reaction of 2-bromo-1,4-dimethylbenzene with $NaNH_2$ in liquid $NH_3$ proceeds via an elimination-addition mechanism involving a benzyne intermediate.

The strong base $NH_2^-$ abstracts the proton from the C3 position, which is the only available position ortho to the bromine atom since C1 is substituted with a methyl group.

Elimination of the bromide ion forms a benzyne intermediate, 1,4-dimethylbenzyne, with the aryne triple bond between C2 and C3.

The nucleophile $NH_2^-$ can attack either C2 or C3 of this intermediate.

Due to the symmetry of 1,4-dimethylbenzyne, attack at either C2 or C3 yields the exact same product, 2-amino-1,4-dimethylbenzene.

This matches the structure given in Option (C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the two main products formed in the following reaction.
questions.solution:
The reaction of $p$-bromotoluene with the strong base $NaNH_2$ in liquid $NH_3$ proceeds via an elimination-addition mechanism, involving a benzyne intermediate.

First, the amide ion ($NH_2^-$) abstracts a proton from the ortho position relative to the bromine atom (which is the meta position relative to the methyl group). This is followed by the elimination of the bromide ion to form the intermediate 4-methylbenzyne.

The "triple bond" in 4-methylbenzyne is located between the meta and para carbons (C3 and C4).

Next, the nucleophile ($NH_2^-$) can attack either of the two carbons involved in the benzyne triple bond:
1. Attack at the para carbon (C4) yields $p$-toluidine.
2. Attack at the meta carbon (C3) yields $m$-toluidine.

Therefore, the reaction produces a mixture of $p$-toluidine and $m$-toluidine as the two main products.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product formed in the following sequence of reactions:


(i) $\mathrm{HNO}_{3} / \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4} / \Delta$
(ii) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{Cl} / \mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$
(iii) $\mathrm{Sn} / \mathrm{HCl}$
(iv) $\mathrm{NaOH} / \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$
questions.solution:
Step 1: The first reaction is the nitration of chlorobenzene using a mixture of $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$. The $-\mathrm{Cl}$ group is an ortho/para-directing group. Due to steric hindrance at the ortho position, the major product formed is 1-chloro-4-nitrobenzene (p-nitrochlorobenzene).

Step 2: The second reaction is Friedel-Crafts alkylation using $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{Cl}$ and $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$. However, the benzene ring now has a strongly deactivating $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ group. Friedel-Crafts reactions do not occur on rings containing strongly deactivating groups. Therefore, no alkylation takes place, and the compound remains p-nitrochlorobenzene.

Step 3: The third step involves reduction using $\mathrm{Sn}$ and $\mathrm{HCl}$. This reagent specifically reduces the nitro group ($-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$) to an amino group, forming the p-chloroanilinium ion ($-\mathrm{NH}_{3}^{+}$).

Step 4: The final step is treatment with aqueous $\mathrm{NaOH}$, which acts as a base to neutralize the acidic anilinium salt, liberating the free amine. The final major product is p-chloroaniline.

Comparing this with the given options, the structure of p-chloroaniline corresponds to Option (C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following reactions is incorrect?
questions.solution:
Ammonolysis of alkyl halides involves the reaction of alkyl halides with ammonia.

Primary alkyl halides undergo nucleophilic substitution to form primary amines. Thus, the reaction in option (D) is correct.

Tertiary alkyl halides are sterically hindered and do not undergo substitution with ammonia. Instead, ammonia acts as a base, leading to an elimination reaction to form an alkene.

Therefore, $\mathrm{Me}_{3}\mathrm{CCl}$ (a tertiary alkyl halide) reacts with $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ to yield $\mathrm{Me}_{2}\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ (elimination product) and not $\mathrm{Me}_{3}\mathrm{CNH}_{2}$ (substitution product).

This makes reaction (A) incorrect and reaction (C) correct.

Answer: $\mathrm{Me}_{3} \mathrm{CCl} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{NH}_{3}} \mathrm{Me}_{3} \mathrm{CNH}_{2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence:


1. $\mathrm{Mg}, \mathrm{Et}_{2}\mathrm{O}$
2. $\mathrm{CD}_{2}\mathrm{O}$
3. $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$
questions.solution:
Step 1: The reaction of 2-bromotoluene with magnesium ($\mathrm{Mg}$) in dry ether ($\mathrm{Et}_{2}\mathrm{O}$) forms the Grignard reagent, 2-methylphenylmagnesium bromide.

Step 2: The Grignard reagent acts as a nucleophile and attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of deuterated formaldehyde ($\mathrm{CD}_{2}\mathrm{O}$). This nucleophilic addition forms an alkoxide intermediate, where the $-\mathrm{CD}_{2}-\mathrm{O}^{-}$ group is attached to the ortho position relative to the methyl group.

Step 3: Hydrolysis of the alkoxide intermediate with water ($\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$) protonates the oxygen atom, converting it into an alcohol ($-\mathrm{OH}$).

The final product is 2-(methylphenyl)methan-d2-ol, which has a $-\mathrm{CD}_{2}\mathrm{OH}$ group ortho to the $-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ group on the benzene ring.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:

$\xrightarrow{\mathrm{BuLi}}$ ?
questions.solution:
The given reaction is an example of the Doering-Moore-Skattebøl rearrangement, which converts a gem-dibromocyclopropane into an allene.

Step 1: When the gem-dibromocyclopropane reacts with $n$-butyllithium (BuLi), a halogen-metal exchange occurs, replacing one of the bromine atoms with lithium to form a lithium carbenoid intermediate.

Step 2: This intermediate undergoes $\alpha$-elimination of LiBr to generate a highly reactive cyclopropylidene (carbene) intermediate at the central carbon atom.

Step 3: The cyclopropylidene undergoes a rapid electrocyclic ring opening. The carbon-carbon single bond opposite to the carbene carbon (the bond connecting the two spiro carbons) breaks.

Step 4: The electrons from this breaking bond rearrange to form two cumulative double bonds with the central carbene carbon, resulting in an allene structure ($>\text{C}=\text{C}=\text{C}<$).

Since the two adjacent carbons in the original central ring are spiro-fused to two other cyclopropane rings, these rings remain intact during the rearrangement. The final product is an allene where the central carbon is double-bonded to the two cyclopropane rings.

This matches the structure given in Option (C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product formed in the following sequence of reactions:



(i) $\mathrm{Li}$, $\mathrm{Et}_{2}\mathrm{O}$
(ii) $\mathrm{HCHO}$, then $\mathrm{H}^{+}$
(iii) $\mathrm{HCl}$
(iv) $\mathrm{Li}$, $\mathrm{Et}_{2}\mathrm{O}$
(v) $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$, then $\mathrm{H}^{+}$
questions.solution:
The given sequence of reactions involves the following steps:

Step 1: Chlorobenzene reacts with lithium ($\mathrm{Li}$) in dry ether ($\mathrm{Et_2O}$) to form phenyllithium ($\mathrm{C_6H_5Li}$).

Step 2: Phenyllithium acts as a nucleophile and attacks formaldehyde ($\mathrm{HCHO}$). Subsequent acidic workup ($\mathrm{H^+}$) yields benzyl alcohol ($\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2OH}$).

Step 3: Benzyl alcohol reacts with hydrochloric acid ($\mathrm{HCl}$) to undergo nucleophilic substitution, producing benzyl chloride ($\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2Cl}$).

Step 4: Benzyl chloride reacts with lithium in dry ether to form benzyllithium ($\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2Li}$).

Step 5: Benzyllithium undergoes nucleophilic addition with carbon dioxide ($\mathrm{CO_2}$). Following acidic workup ($\mathrm{H^+}$), the final product formed is phenylacetic acid ($\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2COOH}$).

The structure of phenylacetic acid matches the one given in option (C).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the given reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The given reaction sequence involves three main steps:

Step 1: The starting material is 2-(3-bromophenyl)-1,3-dioxolane, which is an acetal-protected 3-bromobenzaldehyde. When treated with Mg in ether, a Grignard reagent is formed at the position of the bromine atom. The acetal group is stable under these basic conditions. The intermediate formed is 3-(1,3-dioxolan-2-yl)phenylmagnesium bromide.

Step 2: Ethanol (EtOH) is added. Grignard reagents are strong bases and will react with the acidic proton of the alcohol rather than acting as a nucleophile. This acid-base reaction protonates the aromatic ring, replacing the $-MgBr$ group with a hydrogen atom. The resulting intermediate is 2-phenyl-1,3-dioxolane.

Step 3: The addition of catalytic $H^+$ and $H_2O$ provides acidic hydrolysis conditions. Acetals are readily hydrolyzed back to their corresponding aldehydes and diols in the presence of aqueous acid. The 1,3-dioxolane ring is cleaved to yield benzaldehyde and ethylene glycol.

Therefore, the major product formed is benzaldehyde.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following reactions is not feasible?
questions.solution:
The Finkelstein reaction involves the conversion of an alkyl chloride or bromide to an alkyl iodide by treatment with sodium iodide in dry acetone.

Sodium iodide ($\mathrm{NaI}$) is soluble in acetone, whereas sodium chloride ($\mathrm{NaCl}$) and sodium bromide ($\mathrm{NaBr}$) are insoluble. The precipitation of $\mathrm{NaCl}$ or $\mathrm{NaBr}$ drives the forward reaction according to Le Chatelier's principle.

Thus, the reaction $\mathrm{ClCH}_{2} \mathrm{COOH} + \mathrm{NaI} \xrightarrow{\text{Acetone}} \mathrm{ICH}_{2} \mathrm{COOH} + \mathrm{NaCl} \downarrow$ is feasible.

The reverse reaction $\mathrm{ICH}_{2} \mathrm{COOH} + \mathrm{NaCl} \xrightarrow{\text{Acetone}} \mathrm{ClCH}_{2} \mathrm{COOH} + \mathrm{NaI}$ is not feasible because $\mathrm{NaCl}$ is insoluble in acetone and cannot provide $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ions for nucleophilic substitution.

Answer: $\mathrm{ICH}_{2} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{NaCl} \xrightarrow{\text { Acetone }} \mathrm{ClCH}_{2} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{NaI}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following structures is the most stable?
questions.solution:
The given structures represent the resonance contributors of the Meisenheimer complex (carbanion intermediate) formed during a nucleophilic aromatic substitution ($S_NAr$) reaction.

The stability of these carbanion structures depends on the ability of the substituents to delocalize and stabilize the negative charge. The $-NO_2$ group is a strong electron-withdrawing group that stabilizes negative charge through both the inductive effect ($-I$) and the resonance effect ($-M$).

In structures (A), (B), and (D), the $-NO_2$ group is at the para position relative to the site of nucleophilic attack. These are resonance structures of the same intermediate.

In structure (C), the $-NO_2$ group is at the meta position. From the meta position, the $-NO_2$ group can only provide $-I$ stabilization, as resonance delocalization ($-M$ effect) onto the nitro group is not possible. Thus, the intermediate represented by (C) is less stable.

Comparing the resonance structures (A), (B), and (D):
In structure (D), the negative charge is localized on the carbon atom that is directly attached to the $-NO_2$ group. This specific resonance contributor is exceptionally stable because it allows the negative charge to be directly delocalized onto the highly electronegative oxygen atoms of the nitro group via the $-M$ effect. It also benefits from the strongest $-I$ effect due to the minimum distance between the negative charge and the $-NO_2$ group.

Therefore, structure (D) is the most stable resonance contributor.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the final major product formed when benzene () is subjected to the following sequence of reactions:

(i) $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}, \mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$
(ii) $\mathrm{Mg}, \mathrm{Et}_{2}\mathrm{O}$

(iv) $\mathrm{H}^{+} / \Delta$
questions.solution:
Step 1: Benzene reacts with $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ in the presence of Lewis acid $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ to undergo electrophilic aromatic substitution, forming chlorobenzene ($\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{Cl}$).

Step 2: Chlorobenzene reacts with magnesium ($\mathrm{Mg}$) in dry ether ($\mathrm{Et}_{2}\mathrm{O}$) to form the Grignard reagent, phenylmagnesium chloride ($\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{MgCl}$).

Step 3: The Grignard reagent acts as a nucleophile and attacks the less sterically hindered carbon of the oxirane (ethylene oxide) ring, opening it to form an alkoxide intermediate: $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{O}^{-}\mathrm{MgCl}^{+}$.

Step 4: Upon treatment with acid ($\mathrm{H}^{+}$), the alkoxide is protonated to yield 2-phenylethanol ($\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{OH}$). Heating in the presence of acid ($\Delta$) causes dehydration of the alcohol. The elimination of a water molecule forms a double bond that is conjugated with the benzene ring, resulting in the highly stable product styrene ($\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The reaction of 2-bromo-1,4-dimethylbenzene with $\text{NaNH}_2$ in liquid $\text{NH}_3$ proceeds via a benzyne mechanism.

The strong base $\text{NH}_2^-$ abstracts a proton from the carbon ortho to the bromine atom. Since the C1 position is blocked by a methyl group, the proton is abstracted from C3.

This elimination of $\text{HBr}$ generates a benzyne intermediate with a triple bond between C2 and C3.

Subsequent nucleophilic attack by $\text{NH}_2^-$ can occur at either C2 or C3 of the benzyne intermediate.

Attack at C2 yields 2-amino-1,4-dimethylbenzene, and attack at C3 yields 3-amino-1,4-dimethylbenzene. Because of the symmetry of the molecule, both pathways lead to the same product, 2,5-dimethylaniline.

The structure in option (B) correctly shows the amino group at the C2 position, which corresponds to one of the reactive sites of the benzyne intermediate.
